Antitrust.case: NCAA v. Alston (2021)
Antitrust.summary: The NCAA is not immune from the Sherman Act because its restrictions involve the intersection of higher education, sports, and money.

Antitrust.case: Ohio v. American Express Co. (2018)
Antitrust.summary: Evidence of a price increase on one side of a two-sided transaction platform cannot by itself demonstrate an anti-competitive exercise of market power.

Antitrust.case: North Carolina State Board of Dental Examiners v. FTC (2015)
Antitrust.summary: A non-sovereign actor controlled by active market participants enjoys state action immunity only if the restraint is clearly articulated as state policy and actively supervised by the state.

Antitrust.case: FTC v. Actavis, Inc. (2013)
Antitrust.summary: Reverse payment settlement agreements in the pharmaceutical industry are not automatically illegal but must be analyzed under the rule of reason.

Antitrust.case: American Needle, Inc. v. NFL (2010)
Antitrust.summary: NFL teams are independent economic actors; their joint licensing of intellectual property constitutes concerted activity under the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: Pacific Bell Telephone Co. v. linkLine Communications, Inc. (2009)
Antitrust.summary: Without a duty to deal at the wholesale level or predatory pricing at the retail level, a firm need not maintain rivals' profit margins.

Antitrust.case: Leegin Creative Leather Products, Inc. v. PSKS, Inc. (2007)
Antitrust.summary: Vertical price restraints should be evaluated under the rule of reason.

Antitrust.case: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly (2007)
Antitrust.summary: To claim relief under Section 1 of the Sherman Act, plaintiffs must allege enough factual matter to suggest an agreement was made.

Antitrust.case: Illinois Tool Works, Inc. v. Independent Ink, Inc. (2006)
Antitrust.summary: A patent does not automatically confer market power; plaintiffs must prove market power in tying arrangement cases.

Antitrust.case: Texaco, Inc. v. Dagher (2006)
Antitrust.summary: A lawful joint venture may set its own product prices without violating Section 1 of the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: Verizon Communications, Inc. v. Law Offices of Curtis V. Trinko, LLP (2003)
Antitrust.summary: There is generally no duty to aid competitors under the antitrust laws.

Antitrust.case: California Dental Ass'n v. FTC (1999)
Antitrust.summary: A 'quick look' analysis may be used when the anti-competitive effects of an agreement are obvious even to a lay observer.

Antitrust.case: Brown v. Pro Football, Inc. (1996)
Antitrust.summary: Agreements among employers following an impasse in labor negotiations may be protected from antitrust scrutiny under federal labor laws.

Antitrust.case: Brooke Group Ltd. v. Brown & Williamson Tobacco Corp. (1993)
Antitrust.summary: Predatory pricing claims require proof of below-cost pricing and a reasonable prospect of recouping the losses.

Antitrust.case: Eastman Kodak Co. v. Image Technical Services, Inc. (1992)
Antitrust.summary: A lack of market power in a primary market does not preclude power in derivative aftermarkets.

Antitrust.case: Palmer v. BRG of Georgia, Inc. (1990)
Antitrust.summary: Agreements between competitors to divide markets are per se illegal.

Antitrust.case: FTC v. Superior Court Trial Lawyers Ass'n (1990)
Antitrust.summary: An agreement is not exempt from antitrust laws merely because its goal is favorable legislation.

Antitrust.case: FTC v. Indiana Federation of Dentists (1986)
Antitrust.summary: A professional group's agreement to withhold services desired by customers violates the rule of reason.

Antitrust.case: Matsushita Electrical Industrial Co., Ltd. v. Zenith Radio Corp. (1986)
Antitrust.summary: Antitrust plaintiffs must show evidence that tends to exclude independent action by defendants to survive summary judgment.

Antitrust.case: Aspen Skiing Co. v. Aspen Highlands Skiing Corp. (1985)
Antitrust.summary: A monopolist's refusal to cooperate with a competitor may have evidentiary significance under certain circumstances.

Antitrust.case: Northwest Wholesale Stationers, Inc. v. Pacific Stationery & Printing Co. (1985)
Antitrust.summary: Per se rules apply only when the conduct is likely to have predominantly anti-competitive effects.

Antitrust.case: NCAA v. Board of Regents of University of Oklahoma (1984)
Antitrust.summary: The NCAA's television plan violated Section 1 of the Sherman Act under the rule of reason, though not per se illegal.

Antitrust.case: Jefferson Parish Hospital District No. 2 v. Hyde (1984)
Antitrust.summary: Tying arrangements are unlawful only when they restrain competition on the merits by forcing unwanted purchases.

Antitrust.case: Arizona v. Maricopa County Medical Society (1982)
Antitrust.summary: Agreements among physicians to fix maximum fees are per se illegal price-fixing under Section 1 of the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: Catalano, Inc. v. Target Sales, Inc. (1980)
Antitrust.summary: Competitors' agreements requiring advance payment are per se illegal price-fixing.

Antitrust.case: Broadcast Music, Inc. v. CBS, Inc. (1979)
Antitrust.summary: Blanket licenses issued by rights organizations do not constitute per se unlawful price fixing.

Antitrust.case: National Society of Professional Engineers v. U.S. (1978)
Antitrust.summary: The rule of reason does not allow a defense that competition itself is unreasonable.

Antitrust.case: Continental T.V., Inc. v. GTE Sylvania, Inc. (1977)
Antitrust.summary: Vertical non-price restrictions are judged under the rule of reason.

Antitrust.case: Brunswick Corp. v. Pueblo Bowl-O-Mat, Inc. (1977)
Antitrust.summary: Antitrust plaintiffs must show injury stemming from anti-competitive effects of unlawful conduct.

Antitrust.case: Flood v. Kuhn (1972)
Antitrust.summary: Professional baseball remains exempt from antitrust laws due to congressional acquiescence and stare decisis.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. Topco Associates, Inc. (1972)
Antitrust.summary: Horizontal territorial allocation among competitors is a per se antitrust violation.

Antitrust.case: Brown Shoe Co., Inc. v. U.S. (1962)
Antitrust.summary: Merger legality depends on industry structure, concentration trends, and competitive entry barriers.

Antitrust.case: Klor's, Inc. v. Broadway-Hale Stores, Inc. (1959)
Antitrust.summary: Group boycotts are not excused merely because they target a small business.

Antitrust.case: International Boxing Club v. U.S. (1959)
Antitrust.summary: Championship boxing constitutes a distinct market for Sherman Act purposes.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. E.I. du Pont de Nemours & Co. (1956)
Antitrust.summary: Section 2 of the Sherman Act examines control over prices and competition in the relevant market.

Antitrust.case: Lorain Journal Co. v. U.S. (1951)
Antitrust.summary: Using a monopoly position to eliminate emerging competitors violates Section 2 of the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. Paramount Pictures, Inc. (1948)
Antitrust.summary: Vertical integration in film production and distribution is not per se illegal but may violate antitrust law depending on purpose and effect.

Antitrust.case: Fashion Originators' Guild of America v. FTC (1941)
Antitrust.summary: Practices that tend to create a monopoly and limit public benefit from competition violate the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. Socony-Vacuum Oil Co., Inc. (1940)
Antitrust.summary: Price-fixing agreements in interstate commerce are unlawful per se under the Sherman Act.

Antitrust.case: Interstate Circuit, Inc. v. U.S. (1939)
Antitrust.summary: Conspiracies to restrain commerce may be proven through circumstantial evidence of coordinated conduct.

Antitrust.case: Federal Baseball Club of Baltimore, Inc. v. National League of Professional Baseball Clubs (1922)
Antitrust.summary: Professional baseball games are not interstate commerce and thus fall outside federal antitrust laws.

Antitrust.case: Chicago Board of Trade v. U.S. (1918)
Antitrust.summary: The legality of a restraint depends on whether it promotes or suppresses competition in context.

Antitrust.case: Eastern States Retail Lumber Ass'n v. U.S. (1914)
Antitrust.summary: Coordinated reporting of competing sellers' activities supported an inference of conspiracy to restrain trade.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. American Tobacco Co. (1911)
Antitrust.summary: The Sherman Act prohibits all conduct that falls within its spirit, even if disguised or indirect.

Antitrust.case: Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey v. U.S. (1911)
Antitrust.summary: The Sherman Act bans unreasonable restraints of trade as determined by the rule of reason.

Antitrust.case: Loewe v. Lawlor (1908)
Antitrust.summary: The Sherman Act applies to restraints on interstate commerce even if some acts occur solely within one state.

Antitrust.case: Swift & Co. v. U.S. (1905)
Antitrust.summary: Lawful acts become unlawful when integrated into a common plan to monopolize interstate commerce.

Antitrust.case: U.S. v. E.C. Knight Co. (1895)
Antitrust.summary: The Sherman Act targets monopolies in trade or commerce, not manufacturing.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Seven County Infrastructure Coalition v. Eagle County, Colorado (2025)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: A court reviewing an agency's environmental impact statement (EIS) should ensure it addresses the environmental effects of the project at hand, but the EIS need not address the effects of separate projects, and substantial deference is afforded to the agency on the scope and contents of the EIS.

Climate Change & Environment.case: City and County of San Francisco v. EPA (2025)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: §1311(b)(1)(C) of the Clean Water Act does not authorize the EPA to include 'end-result' provisions in NPDES permits, leaving the determination of necessary steps to meet water quality standards as the EPA's responsibility.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Sackett v. EPA (2023)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Water Act extends only to those wetlands with a continuous surface connection to 'waters of the United States' in their own right, making them indistinguishable from those waters.

Climate Change & Environment.case: West Virginia v. EPA (2022)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Congress did not grant the EPA under Section 111(d) of the Clean Air Act the authority to devise emissions caps based on the generation-shifting approach used in the Clean Power Plan.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Michigan v. EPA (2015)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The EPA strayed beyond the bounds of reasonable interpretation in concluding that cost is not a relevant factor in determining the appropriateness of regulating power plants.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Utility Air Regulatory Group v. EPA (2014)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The EPA exceeded its statutory authority by interpreting the Clean Air Act to require permitting for stationary sources based on their greenhouse gas emissions, though it may require BACT for 'anyway' sources.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Los Angeles County Flood Control Dist. v. NRDC (2013)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The flow of water from an improved portion of a navigable waterway into an unimproved portion of the same waterway is not a 'discharge of a pollutant' under the Clean Water Act.

Climate Change & Environment.case: American Electric Power Co. v. Connecticut (2011)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Air Act and the EPA action it authorizes displace any federal common-law right to seek abatement of carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuel-fired power plants.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Coeur Alaska, Inc. v. Southeast Alaska Conservation Council (2009)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Army Corps of Engineers, rather than the EPA, had the authority to grant permits for the discharge of processed wastewater into an Alaska lake.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Burlington Northern & Santa Fe Railway Co. v. U.S. (2009)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: An entity may qualify as an arranger under CERCLA by taking intentional steps to dispose of a hazardous substance, and apportionment of remediation costs is proper when there is a reasonable basis for determining each cause's contribution to a single harm.

Climate Change & Environment.case: U.S. v. Atlantic Research Corp. (2007)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Section 107(a) of CERCLA provides potentially responsible parties (PRPs) with a cause of action to recover costs from other PRPs.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Environmental Defense v. Duke Energy Corp. (2007)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Air Act does not require the EPA to interpret the term 'modification' consistently in its PSD (Prevention of Significant Deterioration) regulations and its NSPS (New Source Performance Standards) regulations.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Massachusetts v. EPA (2007)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Since greenhouse gases fit within the definition of 'air pollutant' under the Clean Air Act, the EPA has the statutory authority to regulate their emission from new motor vehicles, and a litigant accorded a procedural right by Congress can assert that right in federal court.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Rapanos v. U.S. (2006)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The phrase 'the waters of the United States' includes only relatively permanent, standing, or continuously flowing bodies of water, and only wetlands with a continuous surface connection to these waters are covered by the Clean Water Act.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Cooper Industries, Inc. v. Aviall Services, Inc. (2004)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: A private party that has not been sued under CERCLA Sections 106 or 107(a) may not obtain contribution under Section 113(f)(1) from other liable parties.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Department of Transportation v. Public Citizen (2004)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Since the FMCSA lacks the discretion to prevent the cross-border operations of Mexican motor carriers, neither the National Environmental Policy Act nor the Clean Air Act requires the FMCSA to evaluate the environmental effects of these operations.

Climate Change & Environment.case: South Florida Water Management Dist. v. Miccosukee Tribe (2004)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Under the Clean Water Act, a point source need only convey the pollutant to navigable waters and does not need to be the original source of the pollutant.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Alaska Dept. of Environmental Conservation v. EPA (2004)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Air Act authorizes the EPA to stop the construction of a major pollutant-emitting facility permitted by a state authority when the EPA finds that the authority's BACT (best available control technology) determination was unreasonable.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Whitman v. American Trucking Associations, Inc. (2001)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Section 109(b) of the Clean Air Act does not permit the EPA Administrator to consider implementation costs in setting NAAQS (national ambient air quality standards), and Congress must lay down an intelligible principle when conferring decision-making authority upon agencies.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County v. U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (2001)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Water Act does not allow the Army Corps of Engineers to extend its jurisdiction to wetlands that are not adjacent to open water.

Climate Change & Environment.case: U.S. v. Bestfoods (1998)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: A parent corporation may be charged with derivative CERCLA liability only when the corporate veil may be pierced, but may be directly liable as an operator if it actively participated in and exercised control over its subsidiary's facility operations.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Babbitt v. Sweet Home Chapter of Communities for a Great Oregon (1995)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The definition of “harm” as an expansion of “take” in the Endangered Species Act includes habitat modifications that kill or injure wildlife.

Climate Change & Environment.case: City of Chicago v. Environmental Defense Fund (1994)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: While a resource recovery facility's management activities are excluded from RCRA Subtitle C regulation, its generation of toxic ash is not.

Climate Change & Environment.case: New York v. U.S. (1992)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Constitution grants Congress substantial power to encourage states but does not permit it to commandeer the states' legislative processes by directly compelling them to enact and enforce a federal regulatory program.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Marsh v. Oregon Natural Resources Council (1989)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: An agency must apply a rule of reason and prepare a supplemental environmental impact statement if there remains major federal action to occur, and if new information will significantly affect the quality of the human environment not already considered.

Climate Change & Environment.case: U.S. v. Riverside Bayview Homes, Inc. (1985)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Clean Water Act and Army Corps of Engineers regulations authorize the Corps to require permits for discharging fill material into wetlands adjacent to navigable bodies of water and their tributaries.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Silkwood v. Kerr-McGee Corp. (1984)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The federal preemption of state regulation of the safety aspects of nuclear energy does not extend to a state-authorized award of punitive damages for conduct related to radiation hazards.

Climate Change & Environment.case: EPA v. National Crushed Stone Ass'n (1980)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Federal Water Pollution Control Act does not require the EPA to consider economic capability when granting variances from its uniform BPT (best practicable control technology currently available) regulations.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Strycker's Bay Neighborhood Council v. Karlen (1980)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: Once an agency has made a decision subject to the procedural requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act, a court's only role is to ensure that the agency has considered the environmental consequences.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Vermont Yankee Nuclear Power Corp. v. NRDC (1978)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The concept of alternatives in environmental impact statements must be bounded by feasibility, and Section 553 of the APA establishes the maximum procedural requirements that courts can impose on federal agencies in conducting rulemaking proceedings.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Kleppe v. Sierra Club (1976)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The determination of the region for a comprehensive environmental impact statement is left to the informed discretion of the responsible federal agencies due to the high level of technical expertise required.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Union Electric Co. v. EPA (1976)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: A court of appeals reviewing an EPA-approved state implementation plan cannot set it aside on grounds of economic and technological infeasibility, as Congress intended these factors to be foreign to the EPA's consideration of the plan.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Georgia v. Tennessee Copper Co. (1907)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: A state, in its capacity of quasi-sovereign, has a legitimate interest in demanding that the air over its territory should not be polluted on a great scale by the acts of parties beyond its control.

Climate Change & Environment.case: Missouri v. Illinois (1906)
Climate Change & Environment.summary: The Supreme Court should only intervene to enjoin the action of one state at the demand of another state in cases of serious magnitude that are clearly and fully proved.

Copyrights.case: Warner Chappell Music, Inc. v. Nealy (2024)
Copyrights.summary: A copyright owner with a timely claim for infringement is entitled to recover damages regardless of when the infringement occurred.

Copyrights.case: Andy Warhol Foundation for Visual Arts, Inc. v. Goldsmith (2023)
Copyrights.summary: If an original work and secondary use share the same or highly similar commercial purposes, the first fair use factor will likely weigh against fair use, unless there is another justification for copying.
Copyright protection does not extend to the annotations in the official annotated code of a state.

Copyrights.case: Fourth Estate Public Benefit Corp. v. Wall-Street.com, LLC (2019)
Copyrights.summary: A copyright claimant may commence an infringement lawsuit only after the Copyright Office registers a copyright, but upon registration, the owner can recover damages for infringement occurring both before and after registration.

Copyrights.case: Star Athletica, LLC v. Varsity Brands, Inc. (2017)
Copyrights.summary: A feature of a useful article is eligible for copyright protection only if it can be perceived as a separate two- or three-dimensional work of art and would qualify as a protectable work if imagined separately.

A streaming service infringed the public performance right by selling subscribers a technologically complex service to watch broadcast television programs over the Internet at about the same time as the over-the-air broadcast.

Copyrights.case: Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013)
Copyrights.summary: The first sale doctrine applies to copies of a copyrighted work that were lawfully made abroad.

Copyrights.case: MGM Studios, Inc. v. Grokster, Ltd. (2005)
Copyrights.summary: An entity that distributes a device with the object of promoting its use to infringe copyright, going beyond mere distribution with knowledge of third-party action, is liable for the resulting acts of infringement by third parties.

Copyrights.case: Feltner v. Columbia Pictures Television, Inc. (1998)
Copyrights.summary: The Seventh Amendment provides a right to a jury trial on all issues pertinent to an award of statutory damages in a copyright infringement case, including the amount.

Copyrights.case: Quality King Distributors, Inc. v. L'anza Research Int'l, Inc. (1998)
Copyrights.summary: The first sale doctrine is applicable to imported copies of a copyrighted work.

The harm cognizable under the fourth fair use factor is market substitution, and a parody is unlikely to act as a substitute for the original work because the two usually serve different market functions.

Prevailing plaintiffs and prevailing defendants in copyright infringement cases must be treated similarly regarding attorney fees, which may be awarded to prevailing parties only as a matter of the court's discretion.

Copyrights.case: Feist Publications, Inc. v. Rural Telephone Service Co., Inc. (1991)
Copyrights.summary: The originality requirement necessitates independent creation plus a modicum of creativity, and copyright protection for a compilation of facts extends only to the original components.

Assignees of derivative rights hold only an unfulfilled and unenforceable expectancy if the author dies before the renewal period, unless the assignees secure a transfer of the renewal rights from the author's statutory successor.

Copyrights.case: Community for Creative Non-Violence v. Reid (1989)
Copyrights.summary: A creator was deemed an independent contractor, not an employee, based on factors like engaging in a skilled occupation, supplying own tools, working in a separate workplace, and having control over assistants and working time.
Generally, the author's right to control the first public appearance of their un-disseminated expression will outweigh a claim of fair use.
Congress did not intend to draw a distinction between authorizations to prepare derivative works that are based on a single direct grant and those that are based on successive grants.

Copyrights.case: Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984)
Copyrights.summary: The sale of copying equipment does not constitute contributory infringement if the product is widely used for legitimate, unobjectionable purposes, or is merely capable of substantial non-infringing uses.

Copyrights.case: Twentieth Century Music Corp. v. Aiken (1975)
Copyrights.summary: The reception of a radio broadcast of a copyrighted musical composition is not copyright infringement when the copyright owner has licensed the broadcaster to perform the composition publicly for profit.
The Constitution does not expressly or by inference vest all power to grant copyright protection exclusively in the federal government.
Artistic articles are protected in form by copyright, but not their mechanical or utilitarian aspects.

Copyrights.case: International News Service v. Associated Press (1918)
Copyrights.summary: One who gathers news at pains and expense may be said to have a quasi-property in the results of their enterprise as against a rival in the same business, and the appropriation of those results is unfair competition.
The performance of a copyrighted musical composition in a restaurant or hotel as an incident of other entertainment for which the public pays infringes the public performance right.
The sole right to vend granted by federal copyright law does not secure to the owner the right to qualify future sales by their vendee or to limit or restrict such future sales at a specified price.

The copyright entry of a magazine will not validate the copyright entry subsequently made under a different title by the author of a portion of the contents of the magazine without evidence that the publishers were the assignees or agents of the author for copyrighting.
The least pretentious picture has more originality in it than directories, which may be copyrighted.
Congress can confer copyright protection for photographs that are representations of original intellectual conceptions.
A claim to the exclusive property in a peculiar system of bookkeeping cannot be maintained by the author of a treatise in which that system is exhibited and explained.
No reporter has or can have any copyright in the written opinions delivered by the Supreme Court, and the Justices cannot confer on any reporter any such right.

Criminal Trials/Prosecutions.summary: A criminal defendant is entitled to a new trial when the prosecution knowingly failed to correct false testimony, and this error could have contributed to the verdict.
Brenda Andrew's murder conviction was vacated because the prosecution's use of irrelevant, prejudicial evidence violated her right to a fair trial under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.

When an expert conveys an absent analyst's testimonial statements in support of their opinion, and the statements come into evidence for their truth, the Confrontation Clause will bar their admission.

The Confrontation Clause does not bar the admission of a non-testifying co-defendant's modified confession that avoids directly identifying the non-confessing co-defendant, when accompanied by a limiting instruction.
The Sixth Amendment right to a jury trial requires a unanimous verdict to convict a defendant of a serious offense, and this right is incorporated against the states by the 14th Amendment.
The Sixth Amendment's speedy trial guarantee does not apply once a defendant has been found guilty at trial or has pleaded guilty to criminal charges.
The Confrontation Clause only bars a statement if it was given with the primary purpose of creating an out-of-court substitute for trial testimony.

Out-of-court statements related by an expert solely to explain the assumptions on which their opinion rests are not offered for their truth and thus fall outside the scope of the Confrontation Clause.
If an out-of-court statement is testimonial, it is inadmissible against the accused unless the witness is unavailable and the accused had a prior opportunity to confront that witness.
An identification and description of a shooter and the shooting location were not testimonial statements because their primary purpose was to enable police assistance to meet an ongoing emergency.
Affidavits reporting the results of forensic analysis are testimonial, making the affiants “witnesses” subject to the defendant's right of confrontation under the Sixth Amendment.

A criminal defendant's initial appearance before a magistrate judge, where they learn the charge and their liberty is subject to restriction, triggers the attachment of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel.

The Constitution does not forbid states from insisting on representation by counsel for people competent to stand trial but whose severe mental illness renders them incompetent to conduct trial proceedings by themselves.
A trial court's erroneous deprivation of a criminal defendant's choice of counsel entitles them to reversal of their conviction.

Statements are testimonial for Confrontation Clause purposes when circumstances objectively indicate the primary purpose of the interrogation is to establish past events potentially relevant to later criminal prosecution.
When testimonial statements are at issue, the only indicium of reliability sufficient to satisfy constitutional demands is confrontation.

The right to confront accusatory witnesses may be satisfied without face-to-face confrontation only when necessary to further an important public policy and the testimony's reliability is otherwise assured.

A screen placed between the defendant and the complaining witnesses, which blocked the defendant from their sight, violated the defendant's Sixth Amendment right to confront the witnesses against him.

The Confrontation Clause guarantees an opportunity for effective cross-examination but is satisfied when the defendant has a full and fair opportunity to bring out facts tending to discredit the witness' testimony.

The Confrontation Clause is not violated by the admission of a redacted non-testifying co-defendant's confession that eliminates any reference to the defendant's existence, with a proper limiting instruction.
A defendant makes out a Sixth Amendment violation only by showing that police and their informant took action designed deliberately to elicit incriminating remarks, going beyond merely listening.
The Equal Protection Clause guarantees a criminal defendant that the state will not exclude members of their race from the jury venire or challenge potential jurors solely on account of their race.

A criminal defendant states a violation of the Confrontation Clause by showing they were prohibited from otherwise appropriate cross-examination to show bias, though the improper denial is subject to harmless error analysis.
The Sixth Amendment becomes applicable only when the government's role shifts from investigation to accusation through the initiation of adversary judicial proceedings.
The Sixth Amendment right of a criminal defendant to assistance of counsel is not violated when an attorney refuses to cooperate with the defendant in presenting perjured testimony at their trial.
The right to assistance of counsel attaches at critical stages in the criminal justice process where the results might well settle the accused's fate and reduce the trial itself to a formality.
The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is the right to effective assistance, requiring a defendant to show deficient performance and prejudice that deprived them of a fair trial.
The government violated the Sixth Amendment right to counsel by intentionally creating a situation likely to induce the accused to make incriminating statements without the assistance of counsel.
The Sixth and 14th Amendments require the provision of appointed counsel to an indigent defendant only if they are sentenced to a term of imprisonment.

The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches at or after the initiation of judicial proceedings, and waiver of this right requires the state to prove an intentional relinquishment of a known right or privilege.
The Sixth Amendment guarantees a criminal defendant an independent constitutional right of self-representation when they voluntarily and intelligently elect to do so.
The systematic exclusion of women from jury panels violates the Sixth Amendment requirement that a jury be selected from a representative cross-section of the community.

The right of confrontation is paramount to a state policy of protecting juvenile offenders, outweighing any temporary embarrassment to a witness by the disclosure of their juvenile court record and probation status.

The Sixth Amendment does not grant an accused the right to have counsel present when the government conducts a post-indictment photographic display to allow a witness to attempt an identification of the offender.

A defendant's constitutional right to a speedy trial must be determined on an ad hoc balancing basis weighing the conduct of the prosecution and the defendant, including the length and reason for delay, assertion of the right, and prejudice.
The right of an indigent defendant in a criminal trial to the assistance of counsel is not governed by the classification of the offense or by whether a jury trial is required.
A showup after arrest, but before the initiation of any adversary criminal proceeding, is not a criminal prosecution at which the accused is entitled to counsel as a matter of absolute right.
The Confrontation Clause is not violated by admitting a declarant's out-of-court statements as long as they are testifying as a witness at trial and are subject to full cross-examination.
A defendant's conviction must be set aside on Confrontation Clause grounds when a non-testifying co-defendant's confession inculpating the defendant is introduced, even with a limiting instruction.
The 14th Amendment guarantees a right to a jury trial in all criminal cases that would come within the Sixth Amendment's guarantee, excluding those that qualify as petty offenses.

The Sixth Amendment guarantees an accused the right to counsel at any critical pre-trial confrontation by the prosecution, such as a post-indictment lineup, where the absence of counsel might derogate from their right to a fair trial.
The Sixth Amendment right to confront and cross-examine witnesses is a fundamental right essential to a fair trial and is made obligatory on the states by the 14th Amendment.

When a police investigation focuses on a custodial suspect who has been refused an opportunity to consult with counsel and has not been warned of their right to silence, the accused has been denied the assistance of counsel.

Incriminating statements deliberately elicited by federal agents from a defendant in the absence of their attorney, after indictment, deprived them of their Sixth Amendment right to counsel and could not be used as evidence.
Suppression by the prosecution of evidence favorable to an accused who has requested it violates due process when the evidence is material either to guilt or to punishment.
The right of an indigent defendant in a criminal trial to have the assistance of counsel is a fundamental right essential to a fair trial.
Involuntary verbal confessions are inadmissible in a criminal trial under the Due Process Clause because coerced confessions offend the community's sense of fair play and decency.
Under the circumstances, the refusal of a state court to appoint counsel for an indigent defendant convicted of robbery did not deny him due process of law. (Overruled by Gideon v. Wainwright, 1963)
The right to have the assistance of counsel is too fundamental to be made to depend upon nice calculations by courts of the degree of prejudice arising from its denial.

The right to assistance of counsel may be waived, but the waiver must be intelligent, depending on the particular facts and circumstances, including the background, experience, and conduct of the accused.

The right of the accused, at least in a capital case, to have the aid of counsel for their defense, including sufficient time to advise with counsel, is a fundamental right guaranteed by the Due Process Clause of the 14th Amendment.
A trial for murder in a state court in which the accused are hurried to conviction under mob domination without regard for their rights is without due process and void.
The constitutional guarantee of a jury trial in all criminal prosecutions does not apply to a territory belonging to the U.S. that has not been incorporated into the Union, such as Puerto Rico.

A state law that permits or requires discrimination against a colored man in jury selection because of his race, while securing the right for a white man, denies the colored man equal protection under the law.

Death Penalty and Criminal Sentencing.summary: A sentencer is not required by the Eighth Amendment to make a separate factual finding of permanent incorrigibility before sentencing a juvenile murderer to life without parole.

To establish an Eighth Amendment method of execution claim, a prisoner must show a feasible, readily implemented alternative that significantly reduces a substantial risk of severe pain, which the state refused to adopt without a legitimate penological reason.

The Eighth Amendment may permit the execution of a prisoner who cannot remember committing his crime, but may prohibit executing a prisoner with dementia or another disorder, rather than psychotic delusions.

To succeed in an Eighth Amendment method of execution claim, a prisoner must establish that the method creates a demonstrated and substantial risk of severe pain compared to known and available alternatives.
The Eighth Amendment forbids a sentencing scheme that mandates life in prison without possibility of parole for juvenile homicide offenders.
The Eighth Amendment does not permit a juvenile offender to be sentenced to life in prison without parole for a non-homicide crime.
The Eighth Amendment prohibits the death penalty for crimes against individuals that do not take the victim's life, reflecting the evolving standards of decency that mark a maturing society.
A federal appellate court cannot order an increase in the sentence of a defendant on its own initiative without an appeal or cross-appeal by the prosecution.
To constitute cruel and unusual punishment, an execution method must present a substantial or objectively intolerable risk of serious harm.

A state death penalty statute may constitutionally direct the imposition of the death penalty when the state has proved beyond a reasonable doubt that mitigators do not outweigh aggravators, including when the two are in equipoise.
A state may constitutionally limit the innocence-related evidence that a capital defendant can introduce at a sentencing proceeding to the evidence introduced at the original trial.
The Eighth and 14th Amendments forbid imposition of the death penalty on offenders who were under the age of 18 when their crimes were committed.
Nothing in the Eighth Amendment prohibits a state from choosing to incapacitate recidivist criminals who have already been convicted of at least one serious or violent crime.
Executions of mentally retarded criminals are cruel and unusual punishments prohibited by the Eighth Amendment.
Any fact that increases the penalty for a crime beyond the prescribed statutory maximum, other than the fact of a prior conviction, must be submitted to a jury and proved beyond a reasonable doubt.
The Eighth Amendment does not require strict proportionality between crime and sentence, but only forbids extreme sentences that are grossly disproportionate to the crime.
The Constitution does not require that every finding of fact underlying a sentencing decision be made by a jury rather than a judge.
A complex statistical study indicating a risk of racial bias in capital sentencing determinations does not necessarily prove that a particular defendant's capital sentence was unconstitutional.

The Eighth Amendment does not prohibit the death penalty as disproportionate for a defendant whose participation in a felony resulting in murder is major and whose mental state is one of reckless indifference.
The Eighth Amendment prohibits a state from inflicting the death penalty on a prisoner who is insane.
The Eighth Amendment does not require a state appellate court to compare a death sentence in the case before it with penalties imposed in similar cases before affirming the sentence.

An Eighth Amendment proportionality analysis considers the gravity of the offense and the harshness of the penalty, the sentences imposed on other criminals in the same jurisdiction, and the sentences imposed for the same crime in other jurisdictions.
The death sentence may not constitutionally be imposed after a jury verdict of guilt of a capital offense when the jury was not permitted to consider a verdict of guilt of a lesser included offense.

The Eighth Amendment generally requires that the sentencer must be allowed to consider mitigating factors related to any aspect of a defendant's character, record, or the circumstances of the offense.
The sentence of death for the crime of rape violates the Eighth Amendment.
The punishment of death for the crime of murder does not, under all circumstances, violate the Eighth and 14th Amendments.
The imposition of the death penalty in these cases constituted cruel and unusual punishment in violation of the Eighth Amendment.
In considering the sentence to be imposed after a conviction, the sentencing judge is not restricted to information received in open court, even when a death sentence is imposed.

Due Process.summary: A criminal defendant is entitled to a new trial when the prosecution knowingly failed to correct false testimony, and this error could have contributed to the verdict.

Due Process.case: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022)
Due Process.summary: The Constitution does not confer a right to abortion, overruling *Roe* and *Casey* and returning authority to regulate abortion to the people and their elected representatives.
Due process does not require a state to adopt an insanity test that turns on a defendant's ability to recognize that their crime was morally wrong.
The Excessive Fines Clause of the Eighth Amendment is an incorporated protection applicable to the states under the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause.
The 14th Amendment requires a state to license a marriage between two people of the same sex and to recognize a same-sex marriage lawfully licensed and performed outside the state.

The liberty protected by the Constitution allows homosexual persons the right to choose to enter upon relationships in the confines of their homes and private lives and still retain their dignity as free persons.

Vagueness may invalidate a criminal law if it fails to provide notice enabling ordinary people to understand what conduct it prohibits, or if it authorizes or encourages arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement.

A 'right' to assistance in committing suicide is not a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause; thus, a state law banning physician-assisted suicide does not violate due process.

Due Process.case: Planned Parenthood of Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Casey (1992)
Due Process.summary: An undue burden exists, and therefore a provision of law is invalid, if its purpose or effect is to place substantial obstacles in the path of a woman seeking an abortion before the fetus attains viability.

Due Process.case: Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Dept. of Health (1990)
Due Process.summary: The Constitution does not forbid a state from requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes as to the withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence.

Coercive police activity is a necessary predicate to finding a confession involuntary within the meaning of the Due Process Clause, as the sole concern of the Fifth Amendment is governmental coercion.

Due Process.case: Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill (1985)
Due Process.summary: While the legislature may elect not to confer a property interest in public employment, it may not constitutionally authorize the deprivation of such an interest, once conferred, without appropriate procedural safeguards.
A due process violation does not arise from the unauthorized failure of state agents to follow established state procedure when the state provides an adequate post-deprivation remedy.

Due Process.case: Moore v. City of East Cleveland (1977)
Due Process.summary: When the government intrudes on choices concerning family living arrangements, a court must examine carefully the importance of the governmental interests advanced, as appropriate limits on substantive due process come from careful respect for history and basic societal values.
The Due Process Clause does not require notice and a hearing prior to the imposition of corporal punishment in public schools as that practice is authorized and limited by the common law.

Identifying the specific dictates of due process generally requires considering three factors: the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation and value of additional safeguards, and the government's interest and fiscal/administrative burdens.
Students facing temporary suspension from a public school have property and liberty interests that qualify for protection under the Due Process Clause.

Due Process.case: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez (1973)
Due Process.summary: Strict judicial scrutiny is reserved for cases involving laws that operate to the disadvantage of suspect classes or interfere with the exercise of fundamental rights explicitly or implicitly protected by the Constitution, but poverty is not a suspect class and education is not a fundamental right.

The Due Process Clause protects against state action the right to privacy, including a woman's qualified right to terminate her pregnancy, which the state may not override before viability when its interests in health and potential life become compelling.

Due Process.case: Board of Regents of State Colleges v. Roth (1972)
Due Process.summary: The 14th Amendment does not require a hearing prior to the non-renewal of a non-tenured state teacher's contract unless they can show a deprivation of an interest in liberty or a property interest in continued employment.
From the standpoint of due process, it is immaterial that a deprivation of property may be temporary and non-final.

A pre-termination evidentiary hearing is necessary to provide a recipient of welfare benefits with procedural due process, as the recipient's interest outweighs the state's competing fiscal and administrative concerns.
A statutory scheme to prevent marriages between persons solely on the basis of racial classifications violates the 14th Amendment.
A state law forbidding the use of contraceptives violates the right of marital privacy, which is within the penumbra of specific guarantees of the Bill of Rights.
Suppression by the prosecution of evidence favorable to an accused who has requested it violates due process when the evidence is material either to guilt or to punishment.
The Due Process Clause interposes a bar to a social welfare program only if the statute manifests a patently arbitrary classification, utterly lacking rational justification.

The Due Process Clause no longer should be used to strike down state laws regulatory of business and industrial conditions because they may be unwise, improvident, or out of harmony with a particular school of thought.
Involuntary verbal confessions are inadmissible in a criminal trial under the Due Process Clause because coerced confessions offend the community's sense of fair play and decency.

Due Process.case: Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co. (1950)
Due Process.summary: A fundamental requirement of due process is notice reasonably calculated to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an opportunity to present their objections.

Regulatory legislation affecting ordinary commercial transactions is not unconstitutional unless it is of such a character as to preclude the assumption that the law rests on a rational basis within the knowledge and experience of the legislature.

Due Process.case: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish (1937)
Due Process.summary: A restraint or regulation of the liberty to contract is due process if it is reasonable in relation to its subject and adopted for the protection of the community against evils menacing the health, safety, morals, and welfare of the people.

The Due Process Clause conditions the exertion of regulatory power by requiring that the regulation shall not be unreasonable, arbitrary, or capricious, and that the means selected shall have a real and substantial relation to the object sought to be attained.
The right of the accused, at least in a capital case, to have the aid of counsel for their defense is one of the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Due Process Clause of the 14th Amendment.
A state may provide for the sexual sterilization of inmates of institutions supported by the state who are found to be afflicted with a hereditary form of insanity or imbecility.

The fundamental theory of liberty on which all governments in the U.S. rest excludes any general power of the state to standardize its children by forcing them to accept instruction from public teachers only.
A state law forbidding the teaching of any modern language other than English to a child who has not successfully passed the eighth grade invades the liberty guaranteed by the 14th Amendment.
A trial for murder in a state court in which the accused are hurried to conviction under mob domination without regard for their rights is without due process and void.

The regulation of the working hours of women falls within the police power of the state, and a statute directed exclusively to such regulation does not conflict with the Due Process or Equal Protection Clauses.

A provision that makes it a criminal offense for an interstate carrier to discharge an employee simply because of their membership in a labor organization is an invasion of personal liberty and the right of property and is unenforceable under the Due Process Clause.

The general right to make a contract in relation to one's business is part of the liberty protected by the 14th Amendment, and this includes the right to purchase and sell labor, except as controlled by the state in the legitimate exercise of its police power.

Due Process.case: Chicago, Burlington & Quincy Railroad Co. v. Chicago (1897)
Due Process.summary: A judgment of a state court, even if authorized by statute, whereby private property is taken for public use without compensation made or secured to the owner, is wanting in the due process of law required by the 14th Amendment.
In the privilege of pursuing an ordinary calling or trade, and of acquiring, holding, and selling property, must be embraced the right to make all proper contracts in relation to it.
It is state action of a particular character that is prohibited by the 14th Amendment, and individual invasion of individual rights is not the subject matter of the amendment.

The privileges and immunities of citizens of the United States are those that arise out of the nature and essential character of the national government, the provisions of the Constitution, or federal laws and treaties.

An act of Congress that deprives a citizen of the United States of his liberty or property merely because he came or brought his property into a particular territory of the United States could hardly be dignified with the name of due process of law.

Equal Protection.summary: A Tennessee law banning certain medical care for transgender minors did not violate the Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment, as it satisfied rational basis review.

Equal Protection.case: Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President and Fellows of Harvard College (2023)
Equal Protection.summary: College admissions programs violated the Equal Protection Clause when they lacked sufficiently focused and measurable objectives warranting the use of race, unavoidably employed race in a negative manner, involved racial stereotyping, and lacked meaningful end points.

The 14th Amendment requires a state both to license a marriage between two people of the same sex and to recognize a marriage between two people of the same sex lawfully licensed and performed outside the state.

Equal Protection.case: Schuette v. Coalition to Defend Affirmative Action (2014)
Equal Protection.summary: There is no constitutional authority for the judiciary to set aside state laws that commit to the voters the determination of whether racial preferences may be considered in government decisions.

Strict scrutiny must be applied to any university admissions program using racial categories or classifications, and the university must prove that the means to attain diversity are narrowly tailored to its goal.

Equal Protection.case: Parents Involved in Community Schools v. Seattle School District No. 1 (2007)
Equal Protection.summary: The harm being remedied by mandatory desegregation plans is the harm traceable to segregation, and the Constitution is not violated by racial imbalance in the schools, without more.

The narrowly tailored use of race in admissions decisions to further a compelling interest in obtaining the educational benefits that flow from a diverse student body is not prohibited by the Equal Protection Clause.
The use of standardless manual recounts after a presidential election violated the Equal Protection Clause.

The Equal Protection Clause gives rise to a cause of action on behalf of a 'class of one' when the plaintiff is intentionally treated differently from others similarly situated and there is no rational basis for such treatment.
A state does not violate the Equal Protection Clause by banning assisted suicide while permitting patients to refuse medical treatment.

Parties who seek to defend gender-based government action must demonstrate an exceedingly persuasive justification for that action, which must be genuine and not rely on overly broad generalizations about the sexes.

A state constitutional amendment violated the Equal Protection Clause by precluding all legislative, executive, or judicial action designed to protect the status of persons based on their homosexual, lesbian, or bisexual orientation.

Equal Protection.case: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc. (1985)
Equal Protection.summary: Requiring a special use permit for a proposed group home for mentally retarded people violated equal protection because the difference of the group home was irrelevant to the city's legitimate interests.
A statute will be upheld when the gender classification is not invidious but instead realistically reflects the fact that the sexes are not similarly situated in certain circumstances.

Equal Protection.case: Minnesota v. Clover Leaf Creamery Co. (1981)
Equal Protection.summary: The Equal Protection Clause does not deny a state the authority to ban one type of milk container conceded to cause environmental problems merely because another already established type is permitted to continue in use.

Some state functions are so bound up with the operation of the state as a governmental entity as to permit the exclusion from those functions of all persons who have not become part of the process of self-government.

Equal Protection.case: Regents of Univ. of California v. Bakke (1978)
Equal Protection.summary: A state university may take race into account as a factor in its admissions decisions, but it may not use racial quotas.

Equal Protection.case: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Dev. Corp. (1977)
Equal Protection.summary: Proof of a racially discriminatory intent or purpose is required to show a violation of the Equal Protection Clause.
Classifications by gender must serve important governmental objectives and must be substantially related to the achievement of those objectives.

The judicial scrutiny traditionally devoted to cases involving discrimination along lines of race or national origin is not required when legislation treats legitimate and illegitimate offspring differently.

Equal Protection.case: U.S. Dept. of Agriculture v. Moreno (1973)
Equal Protection.summary: A legislative classification cannot be sustained if it is clearly irrelevant to the stated purposes of the law and does not rationally further any other legitimate government interest.

A statute was unconstitutional when it provided that spouses of male members of the uniformed services would be considered dependents, but spouses of female members would not be considered dependents unless they were in fact dependent for over one-half of their support.

Equal Protection.case: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez (1973)
Equal Protection.summary: Strict judicial scrutiny is reserved for cases involving laws that operate to the disadvantage of suspect classes or interfere with the exercise of fundamental rights and liberties protected by the Constitution.
A ban on distribution of contraceptives to unmarried persons is equally impermissible to a ban on married persons, as the right of privacy inheres in the individual, rather than the marital couple.

Equal Protection.case: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education (1971)
Equal Protection.summary: If school authorities fail in their affirmative desegregation obligations, judicial authority may be invoked, and a district court's equitable powers to remedy past wrongs are broad.
A statutory scheme to prevent marriages between persons solely on the basis of racial classifications violates the 14th Amendment.
The Equal Protection Clause requires substantially equal legislative representation for all citizens in a state, and weighting votes differently according to where citizens reside is discriminatory.

State officials have a duty to obey federal court orders resting on the Supreme Court's interpretation of the Constitution, and state support of segregated schools cannot be squared with the Equal Protection Clause.
The Constitution prohibits the federal government from maintaining racially segregated public schools in the District of Columbia, imposing no lesser duty than on the states.

Equal Protection.case: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka (1954)
Equal Protection.summary: Segregation of children in public schools solely on the basis of race deprives children of the minority group of equal educational opportunities, violating the Equal Protection Clause.

The constitutional guarantee of equal protection is not directed solely against discrimination between whites and African-Americans, and applies when any distinct class is singled out for different treatment not based on a reasonable classification.
The 14th Amendment bars the enforcement of a racially restrictive covenant by an action at law in a state court to recover damages from a co-covenantor for a breach of the covenant.
An African-American student was required to be admitted to a state law school when the separate law school established for African-Americans was not substantially equal to the state law school.
Having been admitted to a state-supported graduate school, an African-American student must receive the same treatment at the hands of the state as students of other races.

Equal Protection.case: Railway Express Agency, Inc. v. New York (1949)
Equal Protection.summary: It is no requirement of equal protection that all evils of the same genus be eradicated or none at all.

It is violative of the Equal Protection Clause for state courts to enforce private agreements to exclude persons of a designated race or color from the use or occupancy of real estate for residential purposes.

Equal Protection.case: Sipuel v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma (1948)
Equal Protection.summary: An African-American person qualified for professional legal education offered by a state cannot be denied such education because of their color, and the state must provide it as soon as it does for applicants of any other group.

All legal restrictions that curtail the civil rights of a single racial group are immediately suspect and must be subjected to the most rigid scrutiny, though pressing public necessity may sometimes justify their existence.

Equal Protection.case: Skinner v. Oklahoma ex rel. Williamson (1942)
Equal Protection.summary: Strict scrutiny of the classification that a state makes in a sterilization law is essential, lest invidious discriminations are made against groups or types of individuals.

Equal Protection.case: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada (1938)
Equal Protection.summary: If a state furnishes higher education to white residents, it is bound to furnish substantially equal advantages to African-American residents, although not necessarily in the same schools.
Regulatory legislation affecting ordinary commercial transactions is not unconstitutional unless it is of such a character as to preclude the assumption that the law rests on a rational basis.

A child of Chinese blood is not denied the equal protection of the law by being classed among the colored races and assigned to separate public schools when equal facilities for education are afforded to both classes.
The regulation of the working hours of women falls within the police power of the state and does not conflict with the Due Process or Equal Protection Clauses.
A law that authorizes or even requires the separation of the two races in public conveyances is not unreasonable.
It is state action of a particular character that is prohibited by the 14th Amendment, and individual invasion of individual rights is not the subject matter of the amendment.

A state law prohibiting a white person and an African-American person from living with each other “in adultery or fornication” did not violate the Constitution even though it prescribed more severe penalties than if they were of the same race.
When the laws of a state recognize and protect the rights of all persons, the 14th Amendment imposes no duty and confers no power on Congress.

When a state law secures to every white man the right of trial by a jury selected from and without discrimination against his race, but permits discrimination against the colored man because of his race, the latter is not equally protected by law.

Free Speech.case: Free Speech Coalition, Inc. v. Paxton (2025)
Free Speech.summary: A law requiring adults to verify their age before they can access speech that is obscene to children is subject only to intermediate scrutiny because it has only an incidental effect on protected speech, and the law at issue met that standard.

The First Amendment offers protection when an entity engaging in expressive activity, including compiling and curating others' speech, is directed to accommodate messages it would prefer to exclude, and a state may not interfere with private actors' speech to advance its own vision of ideological balance.

Free Speech.case: National Rifle Association of America v. Vullo (2024)
Free Speech.summary: The First Amendment prohibits government officials from wielding their power selectively to punish or suppress speech, including through private intermediaries.

When a government official posts about job-related topics on social media, this speech is attributable to the government only if the official possessed actual authority to speak on the government's behalf and purported to exercise that authority when they spoke on social media.
The First Amendment prohibits a state from forcing a website designer to create expressive designs speaking messages with which the designer disagrees.

Although true threats of violence are outside the bounds of First Amendment protection, the First Amendment requires proof of recklessness—that the defendant consciously disregarded a substantial risk that their communications would be viewed as threatening violence.

Free Speech.case: City of Austin v. Reagan National Advertising of Austin (2022)
Free Speech.summary: A distinction between on-premises and off-premises signs was facially content neutral under the First Amendment and thus not subject to strict scrutiny.

Free Speech.case: Manhattan Community Access Corp. v. Halleck (2019)
Free Speech.summary: A private entity operating public access channels on a cable system is not a state actor subject to First Amendment constraints on its editorial discretion, as it is not performing a traditional, exclusive public function.
The state's extraction of agency fees from non-consenting public-sector employees violates the First Amendment.
A ban on voters wearing a political badge, political button, or anything bearing political insignia inside a polling place on Election Day violated the Free Speech Clause.

When an employer demotes an employee out of a desire to prevent the employee from engaging in protected political activity, the employee is entitled to challenge that unlawful action under the First Amendment even if the employer's actions are based on a factual mistake.
Since content-based laws target speech based on its communicative content, they are presumptively unconstitutional and may be justified only if narrowly tailored to serve compelling state interests.
A public employee's sworn testimony outside the scope of their ordinary job duties is entitled to First Amendment protection.

Congress may regulate campaign contributions to protect against corruption or the appearance of corruption, but it may not regulate contributions simply to reduce the amount of money in politics or to restrict the political participation of some.
There is no general exception to the First Amendment for false statements, as some false statements are inevitable if there is to be an open and vigorous expression of views.
Video games qualify for First Amendment protection because, like protected books, plays, and movies, they communicate ideas through familiar literary devices and features distinctive to the medium.
Speech in aid of pharmaceutical marketing is a form of expression protected by the Free Speech Clause.

The First Amendment can serve as a defense in state tort claims, and whether it prohibits holding a defendant liable for their speech turns largely on whether the speech is of public or private concern.

The government may not suppress political speech on the basis of the speaker's corporate identity, as no sufficient governmental interest justifies limits on the political speech of non-profit or for-profit corporations.
The placement of a permanent monument in a public park is a form of government speech and is therefore not subject to scrutiny under the Free Speech Clause.
A principal may restrict student speech at a school event when that speech is reasonably viewed as promoting illegal drug use.
When public employees make statements pursuant to their official duties, the Constitution does not insulate their communications from employer discipline.
When plaintiffs challenge a content-based speech restriction, the government has the burden to prove that the proposed alternatives will not be as effective as the challenged statute.
The governmental interest in preventing the actual or apparent corruption of federal candidates and officeholders constitutes a sufficiently important interest to justify contribution limits.

While a state may ban cross burning carried out with the intent to intimidate, a provision in a statute treating any cross burning as prima facie evidence of intent to intimidate rendered the statute unconstitutional.

Free Speech.case: Republican Party of Minnesota v. White (2002)
Free Speech.summary: A canon of judicial conduct that prohibits a candidate for a judicial office from announcing their views on disputed legal or political issues violates the First Amendment.
A law's reliance on community standards to identify what material is harmful to minors did not by itself render the statute substantially overbroad for First Amendment purposes.

Viewpoint-based funding decisions are improper when the government does not speak or subsidize transmittal of a message that it favors but instead expends funds to encourage a diversity of views from private speakers.

Free Speech.case: National Endowment for the Arts v. Finley (1998)
Free Speech.summary: Although the First Amendment applies in the subsidy context, Congress has wide latitude to set spending priorities, and the consequences of imprecision in decision-making criteria are not constitutionally severe when the government acts as patron.

A law cannot pursue the government's interest in protecting children from potentially harmful materials by suppressing a large amount of speech that adults have a constitutional right to send and receive if less restrictive alternatives would be at least as effective.

Free Speech.case: Rosenberger v. Rector and Visitors of the University of Virginia (1995)
Free Speech.summary: Viewpoint discrimination is presumed impermissible when directed against speech that is otherwise within the limited forum's limitations, and the guarantee of neutrality toward religion is respected when the government extends benefits to recipients whose ideologies, including some that are religious, are broad and diverse.

Free Speech.case: R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992)
Free Speech.summary: Areas of speech that can be regulated because of their constitutionally proscribable content still cannot be made vehicles for content discrimination unrelated to their distinctively proscribable content.
Speech cannot be financially burdened, any more than it can be punished or banned, simply because it might offend a hostile mob.
The enforcement of a public indecency law to prevent totally nude dancing, which is marginally expressive conduct, does not violate the First Amendment guarantee of freedom of expression.
The government may make a value judgment favoring childbirth over abortion and implement that judgment by the allocation of public funds without discriminating on the basis of viewpoint.

In a public forum, the government may impose reasonable restrictions on the time, place, or manner of protected speech, provided the restrictions are justified without reference to the content of the regulated speech, are narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest, and leave open ample alternative channels for communication.

The government generally has a freer hand in restricting expressive conduct than in restricting the written or spoken word, but it may not proscribe particular conduct because it has expressive elements.

Educators do not offend the First Amendment by exercising editorial control over the style and content of student speech in school-sponsored expressive activities, so long as their actions are reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns.
The use of an offensive form of expression may not be prohibited to adults making what the speaker considers a political point, but the same latitude is not permitted to children in a public school.

Free Speech.case: FCC v. League of Women Voters (1984)
Free Speech.summary: A broad ban on all editorializing by every broadcast station that receives public funds exceeds what is necessary to protect against the risk of government interference or to prevent the public from assuming that editorials represent the official view of the government.

When a public employee speaks as an employee on matters only of personal interest, a federal court is generally not the appropriate forum to review the wisdom of a personnel decision taken by a public agency allegedly in reaction to the employee's behavior.

States are entitled to greater leeway in the regulation of pornographic depictions of children, and the *Miller v. California* standard for determining what is legally obscene is not a satisfactory solution to the child pornography problem.

Free Speech.case: Givhan v. Western Line Consolidated School District (1979)
Free Speech.summary: A public employee does not forfeit their First Amendment protection when they arrange to communicate privately with their employer, rather than expressing their views publicly.

Restrictions on individual contributions to political campaigns and candidates did not violate the First Amendment, but restrictions on independent expenditures and limits on candidates' personal expenditures did.

So long as they do not impose liability without fault, states may define for themselves the appropriate standard of liability for a publisher or broadcaster of defamatory falsehood that injures a private individual.

Free Speech.case: Paris Adult Theatre I v. Slaton (1973)
Free Speech.summary: States have a legitimate interest in regulating commerce in obscene material and its exhibition in places of public accommodation, including adult theaters.

The guidelines for obscenity are whether the average person applying contemporary community standards would find the work appeals to the prurient interest, whether it depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way, and whether it lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.

The First Amendment does not relieve a newspaper reporter of the obligation to respond to a grand jury subpoena and answer questions relevant to a criminal investigation, and it does not afford a constitutional testimonial privilege for an agreement to conceal facts.
A state could not make the simple public display of a single four-letter expletive a criminal offense.

Freedoms of speech and press do not permit a state to forbid advocacy of the use of force or of law violation except when such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action.

Free Speech.case: Red Lion Broadcasting Co., Inc. v. FCC (1969)
Free Speech.summary: The purpose of the First Amendment is to preserve an uninhibited marketplace of ideas in which truth will ultimately prevail, and the right of the viewers and listeners, rather than the right of the broadcasters, is paramount.

Free Speech.case: Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969)
Free Speech.summary: A student may express their opinions, even on controversial subjects, if they do so without materially and substantially interfering with the requirements of appropriate discipline in the operation of the school, and without colliding with the rights of others.

When a public employee's false statements concerned issues that were currently the subject of public attention and did not interfere with the performance of their duties or the general operation of their employer, they were entitled to the same protection as if the statements had been made by a member of the general public.

A sufficiently important governmental interest in regulating a non-speech element can justify incidental limitations on First Amendment freedoms if the regulation is within constitutional power, furthers a substantial governmental interest unrelated to the suppression of free expression, and the restriction is no greater than essential.

A public figure who is not a public official may recover damages for defamatory falsehood substantially endangering their reputation on a showing of highly unreasonable conduct constituting an extreme departure from the standards of investigation and reporting ordinarily maintained by responsible publishers.
Allowing unfettered discretion to local officials in the regulation of the use of the streets for peaceful parades and meetings is an unwarranted abridgment of freedom of speech.

Free Speech.case: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan (1964)
Free Speech.summary: A state cannot award damages to a public official for defamatory falsehood related to their official conduct unless they prove actual malice, which means that the statement was made with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard of whether it was true or false.
Obscenity is not within the area of constitutionally protected freedom of speech or press under the First Amendment.

Expression by means of motion pictures is included within the free speech and free press guaranty of the First Amendment, and a state may not place a prior restraint on the showing of a motion picture on the basis of a censor's conclusion that it is sacrilegious.
In the face of a history of tension and violence, a state legislature was not without reason in seeking ways to curb false or malicious defamation of racial and religious groups.
Courts must ask whether the gravity of the evil, discounted by its improbability, justifies such invasion of free speech as is necessary to avoid the danger.
When a speaker passes the bounds of argument or persuasion and undertakes incitement to riot, the police are not powerless to prevent a breach of the peace.

Freedom of speech is protected against censorship or punishment unless it is shown likely to produce a clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil that rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest.

Free Speech.case: West Virginia State Board of Education v. Barnette (1943)
Free Speech.summary: The action of a state in making it compulsory for children in public schools to salute the flag and pledge allegiance violates the First Amendment.

Certain well-defined and narrowly limited classes of speech, including fighting words, which by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace, have never been thought to raise any constitutional problem.

When a clear and present danger of riot, disorder, interference with traffic on the public streets, or other immediate threat to public safety, peace, or order appears, the power of the state to prevent or punish is obvious.
The chief purpose of the free press guaranty is to prevent previous restraints upon publication.
No danger flowing from speech can be deemed clear and present unless the incidence of the evil apprehended is so imminent that it may befall before there is opportunity for full discussion.

The government cannot reasonably be required to defer taking measures against revolutionary utterances advocating the overthrow of organized government until they lead to actual disturbances of the peace or imminent danger of the government's destruction.
It is only the present danger of immediate evil or an intent to bring it about that warrants Congress in setting a limit to the expression of opinion when private rights are not concerned.
The First Amendment, while prohibiting legislation against free speech as such, was not intended to give immunity to every possible use of language.

The delivery of a speech in such word and such circumstances that the probable effect will be to prevent recruiting, and with that intent, is not protected because the purpose to oppose the war was an incidental part of general propaganda.

Words may become subject to prohibition when of such a nature and used in such circumstances as to create a clear and present danger that they will bring about the substantive evils that Congress has a right to prevent.

Government Agencies.summary: Members of the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force within the Department of Health and Human Services are inferior executive officers who may be appointed by the Secretary of Health and Human Services.

Government Agencies.case: Federal Communications Commission v. Consumers' Research (2025)
