Government Agencies.summary: The universal-service contribution scheme did not violate the non-delegation doctrine because Congress provided determinate standards and the agency retained decision-making power while enlisting a private party for recommendations.

Government Agencies.case: Corner Post, Inc. v. Board of Governors (2024)
Government Agencies.summary: An Administrative Procedure Act claim does not accrue for the six-year statute of limitations until the plaintiff is injured by final agency action.

The Chevron deference doctrine is overruled, requiring courts to exercise independent judgment in deciding whether an agency has acted within its statutory authority without deferring to an agency interpretation merely because a statute is ambiguous.

When an agency offers no reasoned response to legitimate concerns posed during the comment period for a rule, this likely indicates that the final rule was not reasonably explained and is arbitrary and capricious.
The authority to modify statutes and regulations allows the Secretary of an agency to make modest adjustments, but not to transform the statutory provisions.
There is a strong presumption in favor of judicial review of final agency action, traditionally available unless a statute's language or structure precludes it.
The Constitution prohibits even modest restrictions on the President's power to remove the head of an agency with a single top officer.

The APA's arbitrary-and-capricious standard requires that agency action be reasonable and reasonably explained, ensuring the agency has acted within a zone of reasonableness and considered the relevant issues.
An agency refusal to reopen a prior benefits determination was subject to judicial review.

Government Agencies.case: Seila Law, LLC v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (2020)
Government Agencies.summary: An independent agency that wields significant executive power and is run by a single individual who cannot be removed by the President unless certain statutory criteria are met clashes with constitutional structure by concentrating power insulated from presidential control.

Government Agencies.case: Dept. of Commerce v. New York (2019)
Government Agencies.summary: Agencies must pursue their goals reasonably, and reasoned decisionmaking under the Administrative Procedure Act calls for an explanation for agency action.

A court should not afford Seminole Rock/Auer deference unless the regulation is genuinely ambiguous after exhausting all the traditional tools of construction, and the agency's reading must still fall within the bounds of reasonable interpretation and warrant controlling weight.
Since an agency is not required to use notice-and-comment procedures to issue an initial interpretive rule, it is also not required to use those procedures to amend or repeal that rule.

The Recess Appointments Clause empowers the President to fill any existing vacancy during any Senate recess of sufficient length, and the Senate is in session when it says that it is, provided it retains the capacity to transact Senate business.
When two halves of a statute do not correspond to each other, this gives rise to an ambiguity that calls for Chevron deference.
Courts must apply the Chevron framework to an agency's interpretation of a statutory ambiguity that concerns the scope of the agency's statutory authority.

Government Agencies.case: Free Enterprise Fund v. Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (2010)
Government Agencies.summary: Multilevel protection from removal is contrary to Article II's vesting of the executive power in the President when the inferior officer determines policy and enforces the laws of the United States.

Government Agencies.case: FCC v. Fox Television Stations, Inc. (2009)
Government Agencies.summary: An agency changing a policy need not demonstrate that the reasons for the new policy are better than the old, but it suffices that the new policy is permissible under the statute, there are good reasons for it, and the agency believes it to be better.
Chevron deference is not accorded merely because a statute is ambiguous, and a rule must be promulgated pursuant to authority that Congress has delegated to the official.

Government Agencies.case: National Cable & Telecommunications Ass'n v. Brand X Internet Services (2005)
Government Agencies.summary: A court's prior construction of a statute trumps an agency construction otherwise entitled to Chevron deference only if the prior court decision holds that its construction follows from the unambiguous terms of the statute and thus leaves no room for agency discretion.

Government Agencies.case: Norton v. Southern Utah Wilderness Alliance (2004)
Government Agencies.summary: A “failure to act” under Section 551(13) of the APA is properly understood as a failure to take one of the agency actions (including their equivalents) earlier defined in that section.
An agency's long-standing interpretation is not automatically deprived of judicial deference because it was previously reached through means less formal than notice-and-comment rulemaking.

Administrative implementation of a statutory provision qualifies for Chevron deference when it appears that Congress delegated authority to the agency generally to make rules carrying the force of law, and the agency interpretation was promulgated in the exercise of such authority.

Government Agencies.case: Whitman v. American Trucking Associations, Inc. (2001)
Government Agencies.summary: The Clean Air Act does not permit the EPA Administrator to consider implementation costs in setting NAAQS, and Congress must lay down an intelligible principle to which the person or body authorized to act is directed to conform.
Agency interpretations in opinion letters, policy statements, agency manuals, and enforcement guidelines are entitled to respect from courts, but only to the extent that they are persuasive.

Government Agencies.case: FDA v. Brown & Williamson Tobacco Corp. (2000)
Government Agencies.summary: In a Chevron analysis, a court must place the statutory provision in context, interpreting the statute to create a symmetrical and coherent regulatory scheme.

Government Agencies.case: Ohio Forestry Ass'n, Inc. v. Sierra Club (1998)
Government Agencies.summary: An agency decision was not ripe for judicial review when withholding review would not cause significant hardship, immediate review could hinder agency efforts, and courts would benefit from further factual development.

Government Agencies.case: National Credit Union Admin. v. First Nat. Bank & Trust Co. (1998)
Government Agencies.summary: In applying the zone of interests test for standing to sue under the APA, a court discerns the interests arguably to be protected by the statutory provision and inquires whether the plaintiff's interests affected by the agency action are among them.

For agency action to be final, it must mark the consummation of the agency's decision-making process and be an action by which rights or obligations have been determined, or from which legal consequences will flow.
The timely filing of a motion to reconsider an administrative order renders the underlying order non-final for the purposes of judicial review.

Federal courts do not have the authority to require a plaintiff to exhaust available administrative remedies before seeking judicial review under the APA when neither the relevant statute nor agency rules specifically mandate exhaustion.
The President is not an agency and is not subject to the APA.

In determining whether exhaustion of administrative remedies is required, a court must balance the individual's interest in prompt judicial access against countervailing institutional interests favoring exhaustion.

Government Agencies.case: Air Courier Conference v. American Postal Workers Union (1991)
Government Agencies.summary: A plaintiff seeking to establish standing to sue under Section 702 of the APA must show that they are within the zone of interests sought to be protected by the statutory provision whose violation forms the legal basis of their complaint.
A party must direct its attack against a particular agency action that causes it harm, rather than seeking wholesale improvement of an agency program by court decree.

Government Agencies.case: Public Citizen v. Dept. of Justice (1989)
Government Agencies.summary: It is unlikely that Congress intended the Federal Advisory Committee Act to cover every formal and informal consultation between the President or an executive agency and a group rendering advice.

Government Agencies.case: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives Ass'n (1989)
Government Agencies.summary: Drug and alcohol tests mandated or authorized by Federal Railroad Administration regulations were reasonable under the Fourth Amendment, even without a warrant or reasonable suspicion, because the compelling government interests outweighed employees' privacy concerns.

Congress did not violate the separation of powers principle by placing the U.S. Sentencing Commission in the judicial branch, requiring federal judges to serve on the Commission, or empowering the President to appoint and remove Commission members for cause.

Congress may place the power to appoint inferior executive officers outside the executive branch and impose a good cause-type restriction on the President's power to remove an official if this does not interfere with the President's exercise of the executive power.
Under the constitutional principle of separation of powers, Congress cannot reserve for itself the power of removal of an officer charged with the execution of the laws except by impeachment.
An agency's decision not to take enforcement action is presumed immune from judicial review under Section 701(a)(2) of the APA.

If a statute is silent or ambiguous with respect to a specific issue, the question for the court is whether the agency's answer is based on a permissible construction of the statute, giving controlling weight to the agency's regulation unless it is arbitrary, capricious, or manifestly contrary to the statute. (Overruled by Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo in 2024)

The presumption favoring judicial review of administrative action may be overcome by inferences of intent drawn from the statutory scheme as a whole, including when a statute provides a detailed mechanism for judicial consideration of particular issues at the behest of particular persons.

Government Agencies.case: Motor Vehicle Mfrs. Ass'n v. State Farm Mutual Automobile Ins. Co. (1983)
Government Agencies.summary: An agency rule would be arbitrary and capricious if the agency has relied on factors that Congress has not intended it to consider, entirely failed to consider an important aspect of the problem, offered an explanation that runs counter to the evidence, or is so implausible that it could not be ascribed to a difference in view or the product of agency expertise.
Congress did not design the Freedom of Information Act exemptions to be mandatory bars to disclosure.

Government Agencies.case: Vermont Yankee Nuclear Power Corp. v. NRDC (1978)
Government Agencies.summary: Section 553 of the APA establishes the maximum procedural requirements that courts can impose on federal agencies in conducting rulemaking proceedings, and a statement of alternatives in an environmental impact statement must be bounded by feasibility.

Identifying the specific dictates of due process generally requires considering the private interest affected, the risk and value of additional procedural safeguards, and the government's interest and administrative burdens.

Restrictions on individual contributions to political campaigns and candidates did not violate the First Amendment, but an appointee exercising significant authority pursuant to the laws of the United States is an “Officer of the United States” and must be appointed in the manner prescribed by the Appointments Clause.

The NLRB is not precluded from announcing new principles in an adjudicative proceeding, and the choice between rulemaking and adjudication lies primarily in the informed discretion of the administrative agency.

Government Agencies.case: U.S. v. Florida East Coast Railway Co. (1973)
Government Agencies.summary: Language in the Interstate Commerce Act providing that an agency may establish reasonable rules “after hearing” did not trigger Sections 556 and 557 of the APA, requiring a trial-type hearing and the presentation of oral arguments by affected parties.
Sections 556 and 557 of the APA need be applied only when the agency statute, in addition to providing a hearing, prescribes explicitly that it be on the record.

Government Agencies.case: Citizens to Preserve Overton Park v. Volpe (1971)
Government Agencies.summary: Arbitrary and capricious review (used for informal rulemaking or adjudication) requires the reviewing court to engage in a substantial inquiry, deciding whether the agency acted within its authority, considered the relevant factors, whether there was a clear error of judgment, and whether the action followed the necessary procedural requirements.
A pre-termination evidentiary hearing is necessary to provide a recipient of welfare benefits with procedural due process, as the recipient's interest clearly outweighs the state's competing concern.

The required records doctrine under Shapiro does not apply when a party was not obliged to keep and preserve records of the same kind as they have customarily kept, there are no public aspects to the information, and the requirements are directed to a selective group inherently suspect of criminal activities.

The Fourth Amendment bars prosecution of a person who has refused to permit a warrantless code enforcement inspection of their personal residence, as warrantless administrative searches cannot be justified on grounds of minimal demands, infeasibility of warrants, or inability of area inspection programs to function under reasonable search warrant requirements.

Courts should restrict access to judicial review of an agency regulation only upon a showing of clear and convincing evidence of a contrary legislative intent, and ripeness requires evaluation of the fitness of the issues for judicial decision and the hardship of withholding court consideration.
A hearing requirement should not be read as withdrawing from the power of an agency the rulemaking authority necessary for the orderly conduct of its business.

A reviewing court is not barred from setting aside an agency decision when it cannot conscientiously find that the evidence supporting that decision is substantial, when viewed in the light that the record in its entirety furnishes, including the body of evidence opposed to the agency view.

The privilege against compelled self-incrimination cannot be maintained in relation to records required by law to be kept in order that there may be suitable information of transactions that are the appropriate subjects of governmental regulation.

The choice between proceeding by general rule or by ad hoc decisions lies primarily in the informed discretion of the administrative agency, and the retroactive effect of an ad hoc decision does not necessarily render it invalid.

Government Agencies.case: Bowles v. Seminole Rock & Sand Co. (1945)
Government Agencies.summary: In interpreting an administrative regulation, a court must look to the administrative construction of the regulation if the meaning of the words used is in doubt, and the administrative interpretation holds controlling weight unless it is plainly erroneous or inconsistent with the regulation.

Rulings, interpretations, and opinions of agency administrators are not controlling on courts but provide a body of experience and informed judgment that courts can use for guidance, with the weight depending on persuasiveness factors.

When a question involves the specific application of a broad statutory term in a proceeding in which the agency administering the statute must determine it initially, the agency determination must be accepted if it has warrant in the record and a reasonable basis in law.

The essentials of the legislative function are preserved when Congress has specified the basic conditions of fact upon whose existence or occurrence, ascertained from relevant data by a designated administrative agency, it directs that its statutory command shall be effective.
An administrative order cannot be upheld unless the grounds on which the agency acted in exercising its powers were those on which its action can be sustained.
Substantial evidence (the standard of review for formal rulemaking or adjudication) means relevant evidence that a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a conclusion.

The authority of Congress in creating quasi-legislative or quasi-judicial agencies to require their officers to act independently of executive control includes the power to fix the period during which they shall continue in office, and to forbid their removal except for cause in the meantime.

Government Agencies.case: A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. U.S. (1935)
Government Agencies.summary: A law was an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power when it authorized the making of codes to prescribe rules of conduct and set up no standards for that legislative undertaking.

An attempted delegation is plainly void when the power sought to be delegated is legislative power, yet nowhere in the statute has Congress declared or indicated any policy or standard to guide or limit the President when acting under such delegation.

The use of the administrative method for determining facts (assuming due notice and opportunity to be heard and that findings are based on evidence) is consistent with due process and is not an unconstitutional invasion of the judicial power.
The President has the exclusive power of removing executive officers of the United States whom he has appointed by and with the advice and consent of the Senate.

Government Agencies.case: Bi-Metallic Investment Co. v. State Board of Equalization of Colorado (1915)
Government Agencies.summary: When a rule of conduct applies to more than a few people, it is impracticable that everyone should have a direct voice in its adoption, and thus an order increasing the valuation of taxable property in a city did not violate due process just because no opportunity was given to the taxpayers to be heard.

When the legislature commits the determination of the tax to a subordinate body, due process requires that a taxpayer be afforded a hearing, of which they must have notice, and the opportunity to support their objections by argument and proof.

Gun Rights.case: Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. v. Estados Unidos Mexicanos (2025)
Gun Rights.summary: The government of Mexico failed to plausibly allege that seven American gun manufacturers aided and abetted unlawful sales routing guns to Mexican drug cartels.
The Gun Control Act of 1968 permits the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives to regulate some weapon parts kits and unfinished frames or receivers.
An individual found by a court to pose a credible threat to the physical safety of another person may be temporarily disarmed consistent with the Second Amendment.

A bump stock does not convert a semiautomatic rifle into a 'machinegun' for the purposes of federal gun control laws, as it does not allow the shooter to achieve a high rate of fire with a single function of the trigger.

Gun Rights.case: New York State Rifle & Pistol Association, Inc. v. Bruen (2022)
Gun Rights.summary: To justify a firearm regulation, the government must demonstrate that the regulation is consistent with the nation's historical tradition of firearm regulation, which is the test for presumptively protected conduct under the Second Amendment's plain text.
The Second Amendment extends to all instruments that constitute bearable arms, even those not in existence at the time of the founding, and this right is fully applicable to the states.
The 14th Amendment incorporates the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms for the purpose of self-defense, protecting the right from state as well as federal interference.

The Second Amendment protects an individual right to possess a firearm unconnected with service in a militia, and to use that arm for traditionally lawful purposes, such as self-defense within the home.
Congress could rationally conclude that any felony conviction, even an allegedly invalid conviction, is a sufficient basis on which to prohibit the possession of a firearm.

A court cannot say that the Second Amendment guarantees the right to keep and bear an instrument, such as a short-barreled shotgun, in the absence of evidence showing a reasonable relationship to the preservation or efficiency of a well-regulated militia.

States cannot prohibit the people from keeping and bearing arms so as to deprive the United States of their rightful military resource, but state legislatures may enact statutes to control and regulate all organizations, drilling, and parading of military bodies and associations unless authorized by federal militia laws.
The Second Amendment's declaration that the right to bear arms shall not be infringed only restricts the powers of the national government.

Healthcare.summary: Members of the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force within the Department of Health and Human Services are inferior executive officers who may be appointed by the Secretary of Health and Human Services.

Healthcare.case: Medina v. Planned Parenthood South Atlantic (2025)
Healthcare.summary: Individual Medicaid beneficiaries do not have a right to sue state officials for failing to comply with Medicaid's any-qualified-provider provision.

Healthcare.case: Little Sisters of the Poor Saints Peter and Paul Home v. Pennsylvania (2020)
Healthcare.summary: The Department of Health and Human Services may promulgate exemptions to the Affordable Care Act to allow for-profit organizations to exempt themselves from the contraceptive coverage requirement on religious or moral grounds.
Congress intended the tax credits authorized under the Affordable Care Act to be available through both state and federal exchanges.

Healthcare.case: North Carolina State Board of Dental Examiners v. FTC (2015)
Healthcare.summary: A non-sovereign actor controlled by active market participants enjoys state action antitrust immunity only if the challenged restraint is clearly articulated as state policy and actively supervised by the state.

Healthcare.case: Burwell v. Hobby Lobby Stores, Inc. (2014)
Healthcare.summary: The Religious Freedom Restoration Act (RFRA) permits a closely held for-profit corporation to deny its employees contraceptive health coverage based on the religious objections of the owners, protecting the religious liberty of the people who own and control them.
Design defect claims based on generic drugs are preempted by federal law.
Reverse payment settlement agreements in the pharmaceutical industry are not per se illegal but should be analyzed according to the rule of reason.

Healthcare.case: National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius (2012)
Healthcare.summary: The individual health insurance mandate under the Affordable Care Act was a permissible use of Congress' taxing power, but the Medicaid funding expansion was an invalid use of the spending power, and the Commerce Clause does not give Congress the power to compel commerce.
Failure-to-warn claims based on generic drugs are preempted by the FDA's interpretation of federal drug regulations.
State tort law failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by the FDCA with regard to prescription drugs, as they serve as an additional safeguard for consumers that complements the goals of the FDA.

The Medical Device Amendments to the Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act preempt state common-law claims challenging the safety or effectiveness of medical devices marketed in a form that received pre-market approval from the FDA.

Chevron deference is not accorded merely because a statute is ambiguous, and a rule must be promulgated pursuant to authority that Congress has delegated to the official; a doctor can prescribe a drug under the Controlled Substances Act for assisted suicide if allowed under state law.
State laws permitting the medical use of marijuana do not prevent Congress from prohibiting its use for any purpose in those states under the Commerce Clause.

Juan Davila sued his HMO under Texas law for denying treatment, but the HMO argued ERISA should apply instead. The Court unanimously ruled that ERISA overrides state laws, preventing such state lawsuits against HMOs.

Healthcare.case: Rush Prudential HMO, Inc. v. Moran (2002)
Healthcare.summary: HMOs may be insurers and thus subject to state regulation of insurers, and state laws directed toward the insurance industry are saved from preemption under ERISA's saving clause.
Mixed treatment and eligibility decisions by health maintenance organization (HMO) physicians are not fiduciary decisions under ERISA.

A “right” to assistance in committing suicide is not a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause, and thus a state law banning physician-assisted suicide does not violate due process.
A state does not violate the Equal Protection Clause by banning assisted suicide while permitting patients to refuse medical treatment.

Healthcare.case: Inter-Modal Rail Employees Ass'n v. Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe Railway Co. (1997)
Healthcare.summary: The power to amend or abolish an employee welfare benefit plan does not include the power to discriminate against the plan's participants and beneficiaries for the purpose of interfering with their attainment of rights under the plan.

Healthcare.case: New York State Conference of Blue Cross & Blue Shield Plans v. Travelers Ins. Co. (1995)
Healthcare.summary: A law operating as an indirect source of merely economic influence on administrative decisions should not suffice to trigger ERISA preemption.

ERISA's deemer clause excludes self-funded ERISA plans from the reach of the saving clause by relieving them from state laws purporting to regulate insurance, so state laws directed toward such plans are preempted.

Healthcare.case: Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Dept. of Health (1990)
Healthcare.summary: The Constitution does not forbid a state from requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes as to the withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence.

The Free Exercise Clause does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a law that incidentally forbids or requires an act that their religious belief requires or forbids if the law is not specifically directed to religious practice and is otherwise constitutional.

Healthcare.case: Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch (1989)
Healthcare.summary: A 'participant' in an ERISA plan means an employee in currently covered employment, or a former employee who has a reasonable expectation of returning to covered employment or who has a colorable claim to vested benefits.

Healthcare.case: Fort Halifax Packing Co., Inc. v. Coyne (1987)
Healthcare.summary: To be preempted by ERISA, a state statute must have some connection with or reference to an employee benefit plan.

Healthcare.case: Pilot Life Ins. Co. v. Dedeaux (1987)
Healthcare.summary: ERISA preempts state common law tort and contract actions asserting improper processing of a claim for benefits under an insured employee benefit plan.

Requiring healthcare providers to store the private information of patients who receive prescriptions for drugs that can be illegally abused is permissible, despite the privacy rights of the patients.
The government may restrict parental authority in the interests of child health and welfare.

City ordinances that require that students be vaccinated to attend school and that vest broad discretion in health authorities to determine when and under which circumstances such a requirement will be enforced do not violate the 14th Amendment.

A state may enact a compulsory vaccination law, since the legislature has the discretion to decide whether vaccination is the best way to prevent smallpox and protect public health, and a differential exemption for children does not violate the equal protection rights of adults.

Immigration and National Security.summary: A citizen does not have a fundamental liberty interest in their noncitizen spouse being admitted to the country.

A federal appeals court may review a hardship determination by an immigration judge deciding whether a non-citizen is eligible for cancellation of removal and adjustment to lawful permanent resident status.

The government is not required to provide foreign nationals detained for six months with bond hearings in which the government bears the burden of proving, by clear and convincing evidence, that a foreign national poses a flight risk or a danger to the community.

A non-permanent resident seeking to cancel a lawful removal order has not met the burden of showing that they have not been convicted of a disqualifying offense when the record of conviction is ambiguous.

Immigration and National Security.case: DHS v. Regents of the University of California (2020)
Immigration and National Security.summary: The DHS decision to rescind the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program was arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act.

The President has broad discretion, vested by Congress, to suspend the entry of foreign nationals into the U.S., including decisions of whether and when to suspend entry, whose entry to suspend, for how long, and on what conditions.
references: Executive Power Clause,Necessary and Proper Clause,All legislative Powers,Commerce Clause

Immigration and National Security.summary: Detained foreign nationals do not have a right to periodic bond hearings during the course of their detention.
The proper balance between deterring constitutional violations and freeing high officials to protect the nation in times of great peril is for Congress to undertake.
A state offense is an aggravated felony for immigration purposes when it has every element of a listed federal crime, except an element requiring a connection to interstate or foreign commerce.

The Court has often found a lack of standing in cases in which the judiciary has been requested to review actions of the political branches in the fields of intelligence gathering and foreign affairs.
Certain provisions of an Arizona law affecting undocumented immigrants were preempted by federal law.
The impact of travel abroad on the permanent resident status of a foreign national with a criminal conviction is determined by the legal regime in force at the time of the conviction.
An Arizona law instructing courts to suspend or revoke the business licenses of in-state employers that employ unauthorized aliens was not preempted by federal law.
Respect for the government's conclusions is appropriate in the area of national security due to the courts' lack of competence in collecting evidence and drawing factual inferences.
A criminal defense attorney must inform a non-citizen client whether their plea carries a risk of deportation.

Courts must accord proper deference to the political branches in imposing detention to prevent acts of terrorism, but security subsists in fidelity to freedom's first principles, such as freedom from arbitrary and unlawful restraint.
Conduct that is a felony under state law but a misdemeanor under the federal Controlled Substances Act is not a felony punishable under the Controlled Substances Act for INA purposes.
The executive must comply with the prevailing rule of law, including the requirements of the Geneva Conventions and UCMJ, in undertaking to try a detainee and subject him to criminal punishment.
Due process demands that a citizen held in the U.S. as an enemy combatant be given a meaningful opportunity to contest the factual basis for that detention before a neutral decisionmaker.
U.S. courts have jurisdiction to consider challenges to the legality of the detention of foreign nationals captured abroad in connection with hostilities and incarcerated at Guantanamo Bay.

Although the Fifth Amendment entitles foreign nationals to due process in deportation proceedings, detention during these proceedings is a constitutionally valid aspect of the process, even without a finding that the foreign national is unlikely to appear for deportation proceedings.
The post-removal period detention statute implicitly limits the detention of a foreign national to a period reasonably necessary to bring about their removal and does not permit indefinite detention.

The Suspension Clause requires some judicial intervention in deportation cases, and Habeas courts have jurisdiction to answer questions of law arising in challenges to executive interpretations of immigration law and the discretionary relief context.

When a foreign national's continuing presence in the U.S. violates immigration laws, the government does not offend the Constitution by deporting the foreign national for the additional reason that they are believed to be a member of an organization that supports terrorist activity.
A guerrilla organization's attempt to coerce a person into performing military service does not necessarily constitute persecution on account of political opinion.
The Fourth Amendment does not apply to the search and seizure by U.S. agents of property owned by a non-resident foreign national that is located in a foreign country.
The 'clear probability' standard of proof for withholding of removal does not govern asylum applications.

Consistent with the civil nature of a deportation proceeding, various protections that apply in the context of a criminal trial do not apply in a deportation hearing, including the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule.
Individual questioning of employees by INS agents concerning their citizenship did not amount to a detention or seizure under the Fourth Amendment.

The role of the judiciary is limited to determining whether the procedures used in excluding a returning lawful permanent resident meet the essential standard of fairness under the Due Process Clause.

A state law violated the Equal Protection Clause when it withheld from local school districts any state funds for the education of children who were not legally admitted into the U.S. and authorized local school districts to deny enrollment to these children.

There must be strict compliance with all the congressionally imposed prerequisites to the acquisition of citizenship, and failure to comply with any of these conditions renders the certificate of citizenship illegally procured and subject to being set aside.
In establishing loss of citizenship, the government must prove an intent to surrender U.S. citizenship, rather than just the voluntary commission of an expatriating act.

Provisions of the Immigration and Nationality Act are not unconstitutional for excluding the relationship between an illegitimate child and their natural father from the special preference status accorded by the INA.

The fact that a federal law treats foreign nationals differently from citizens does not by itself imply that such disparate treatment is invidious, as Congress has broad power over immigration and naturalization.

When the Attorney General decides for a legitimate and bona fide reason not to waive the statutory exclusion of a foreign national, courts will not look behind this decision or weigh it against the First Amendment interests of those who would personally communicate with the foreign national.

State statutes that deny welfare benefits to resident foreign nationals, or to foreign nationals who have not resided in the U.S. for a specified number of years, violate the Equal Protection Clause and encroach on the exclusive federal power over the entrance and residence of foreign nationals.
Congress has no power under the Constitution to divest a person of their U.S. citizenship, absent their voluntary renunciation of it.
Citizenship is not subject to the general powers of the national government, and therefore it cannot be divested in the exercise of those powers.
Congress had the authority under its power to regulate foreign relations to provide that anyone who votes in a foreign political election shall lose their U.S. citizenship.

Immigration and National Security.case: Shaughnessy v. U.S. ex rel. Mezei (1953)
Immigration and National Security.summary: Whatever the procedure authorized by Congress for a foreign national denied entry is, it is due process as far as the foreign national is concerned when they are on the threshold of initial entry.
Even the most compelling necessity cannot overcome a claim of the military secrets privilege if the court is ultimately satisfied that military secrets are at stake.
A returning resident foreign national is entitled as a matter of due process to a hearing on the charges underlying any attempt to exclude them.

Immigration and National Security.case: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer (Jackson concurrence) (1952)
Immigration and National Security.summary: The President's authority varies depending on whether he acts with express or implied authorization from Congress (maximum), in absence of congressional action (zone of twilight), or against the express or implied will of Congress (lowest ebb).

Congress could authorize the deportation of a legally resident foreign national because of membership in the Communist Party, and immigration policy is so exclusively entrusted to the political branches that it is largely immune from judicial inquiry.

Immigration and National Security.case: U.S. ex rel. Knauff v. Shaughnessy (1950)
Immigration and National Security.summary: Admission of foreign nationals to the U.S. is a privilege granted by the sovereign U.S. government, and it is not within the province of any court to review the determination of the political branch of the government to exclude a foreign national.
A foreign national who is willing to take the oath of allegiance and to serve as a non-combatant but is unwilling to bear arms due to religious scruples may be admitted to citizenship.

When an indictment charges treason by giving aid and comfort to enemies of the U.S., this requires proof of an overt act by the accused that is sufficient in its setting to sustain a finding that they actually gave aid and comfort to the enemy.

All legal restrictions that curtail the civil rights of a single racial group are immediately suspect and must be subjected to the most rigid scrutiny, but pressing public necessity may sometimes justify the existence of such restrictions.

The Fifth and Sixth Amendments do not abolish trials by military tribunals or impose on these tribunals the necessity of proceeding against unlawful enemy belligerents only on presentment and trial by jury.

Congressional legislation in the international field must often accord to the President a degree of discretion and freedom from statutory restriction that would not be admissible were domestic affairs alone involved.
The grant to Congress of the power to raise and support armies includes the power to compel military service.

Deportation is not a punishment but instead a refusal by the government to harbor persons whom it does not want, and the constitutional prohibition on ex post facto laws has no application to deportation.
references: Bills of Attainder; Ex Post Facto Laws

Immigration and National Security.summary: When a foreign national had notice, although not formal, of the investigation instituted for the purpose of ascertaining whether they were illegally in the country, the courts cannot interfere with the executive officers conducting it.

The 14th Amendment affirms the rule of citizenship by birth within the territory, in the allegiance, and under the protection of the U.S., including most children born here to resident foreign nationals.

All persons within the territory of the U.S. are entitled to the protections of the Fifth and Sixth Amendments, including foreign nationals who cannot be deprived of life, liberty, or property without due process of law or be held to answer for a “capital or other infamous crime” without a grand jury indictment.

Deciding whether foreign nationals may be permitted to stay in the U.S. falls within the authority of the political departments of the government, and courts cannot express opinions on the wisdom, policy, or justice of measures enacted by Congress in this area.

It is not within the province of the judiciary to order that foreigners who have never been naturalized, nor acquired any domicile or residence within the U.S., shall be permitted to enter in opposition to the constitutional and lawful measures of the legislative and executive branches of the national government.
The power of Congress to exclude foreign nationals from the U.S. is an incident of sovereignty that cannot be surrendered by the treaty-making power.

The 14th Amendment extends to all persons within the territorial jurisdiction of the U.S., and thus foreign nationals who have the right to temporarily or permanently reside in the U.S. are entitled to equal protection.
The two sources of citizenship are birth and naturalization, and people not subject to the jurisdiction of the U.S. at the time of birth cannot become so afterwards except by being naturalized.

Labor and Employment.case: Stanley v. City of Sanford, Florida (2025)
Labor and Employment.summary: The employment discrimination provision of the Americans With Disabilities Act does not reach discrimination against retirees who neither hold nor desire a job whose essential tasks they can perform with reasonable accommodation.

Labor and Employment.case: Ames v. Ohio Department of Youth Services (2025)
Labor and Employment.summary: For a prima facie case of disparate treatment employment discrimination under Title VII, a plaintiff who is a member of a majority group does not need to show background circumstances to support the suspicion that the defendant is an unusual employer who discriminates against the majority.
The traditional four-factor test for a preliminary injunction governs NLRB requests for a preliminary injunction from a federal district court while administrative enforcement proceedings take place.

Labor and Employment.case: Muldrow v. City of St. Louis, Missouri (2024)
Labor and Employment.summary: Although an employee must show some harm from a forced job transfer to prevail in a Title VII claim, they do not need to show that the injury satisfies a significance test.

Title VII requires an employer that denies a religious accommodation to show that the burden of granting an accommodation would result in substantial increased costs in relation to the conduct of its particular business, and the impact on coworkers is relevant only to the extent that it affects the conduct of the business.
An employer that fires an individual merely for being gay or transgender violates Title VII.
The federal-sector provision of the ADEA demands that personnel actions be untainted by any consideration of age.
The state's extraction of agency fees from non-consenting public-sector employees violates the First Amendment.

Labor and Employment.case: Digital Reality Trust, Inc. v. Somers (2018)
Labor and Employment.summary: Dodd-Frank's anti-retaliation provision does not extend to an individual who has not reported a violation of the securities laws to the SEC.

When an employer demotes an employee out of a desire to prevent the employee from engaging in protected political activity, the employee is entitled to challenge that unlawful action under the First Amendment even if the employer's actions are based on a factual mistake.

Labor and Employment.case: Gobeille v. Liberty Mutual Ins. Co. (2016)
Labor and Employment.summary: ERISA preempts a state law that governs or interferes with the uniformity of plan administration.

Labor and Employment.case: EEOC v. Abercrombie & Fitch Stores, Inc. (2015)
Labor and Employment.summary: To prevail in a disparate treatment claim, an applicant needs to show only that their need for a religious accommodation was a motivating factor in the employer's decision, not that the employer had knowledge of their need.

Labor and Employment.case: Integrity Staffing Solutions, Inc. v. Busk (2014)
Labor and Employment.summary: Time spent waiting to undergo and undergoing security screenings is not compensable under the FLSA.
A public employee's sworn testimony outside the scope of their ordinary job duties is entitled to First Amendment protection.

Labor and Employment.case: Univ. of Texas Southwestern Medical Center v. Nassar (2013)
Labor and Employment.summary: Title VII retaliation claims must be proved according to traditional principles of but-for causation.
An employee is a supervisor for the purposes of vicarious liability under Title VII only if they are empowered by the employer to take tangible employment actions against the victim.

Labor and Employment.case: Hosanna-Tabor Evangelical Lutheran Church and School v. EEOC (2012)
Labor and Employment.summary: The Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses bar lawsuits brought on behalf of ministers against their churches, claiming termination in violation of employment discrimination laws.

A government employer's allegedly retaliatory actions against an employee do not give rise to liability under the Petition Clause of the First Amendment unless the employee's petition relates to a matter of public concern.

A class in a proposed employment discrimination class action has common questions of law or fact if their claims depend on a common contention of such a nature that determination of its truth or falsity will resolve an issue that is central to the validity of each of the claims in one stroke.

If a supervisor performs an act motivated by discriminatory animus that is intended by the supervisor to cause an adverse employment action, and if that act is a proximate cause of the ultimate employment action, the employer is liable.
A government employer had a right to read text messages sent and received on a pager that the employer owned and issued to an employee.

Before an employer can engage in intentional discrimination for the asserted purpose of avoiding an unintentional disparate impact, the employer must have a strong basis in evidence to believe that it will be subject to disparate impact liability if it fails to take the race-conscious, discriminatory action.

Labor and Employment.case: Gross v. FBL Financial Services, Inc. (2009)
Labor and Employment.summary: A plaintiff bringing an ADEA disparate treatment claim must prove that age was the but-for cause of the adverse employment action.
A provision in a collective bargaining agreement that clearly and unmistakably requires union members to arbitrate ADEA claims is enforceable as a matter of federal law.

Labor and Employment.case: Meacham v. Knolls Atomic Power Laboratory (2008)
Labor and Employment.summary: An employer defending a disparate impact claim under the ADEA bears both the burden of production and the burden of persuasion for the “reasonable factors other than age” (RFOA) affirmative defense.

Labor and Employment.case: Burlington Northern & Santa Fe Railway Co. v. White (2006)
Labor and Employment.summary: The anti-retaliation provision of Title VII covers only those employer actions that would have been materially adverse to a reasonable employee or applicant, meaning the action might have dissuaded a reasonable worker from making or supporting a charge of discrimination.
When public employees make statements pursuant to their official duties, the Constitution does not insulate their communications from employer discipline.

The time that employees spend walking between the changing area and the production area is compensable under the FLSA when they must don protective clothing on the employer's premises before engaging in productive labor, but the time spent waiting to put on the protective gear is not compensable.

While the ADEA authorizes recovery in disparate impact cases, the statute significantly narrows its coverage by permitting any otherwise prohibited action when the differentiation is based on reasonable factors other than age.

A plaintiff alleging sexual harassment can establish constructive discharge if the abusive working environment became so intolerable that resignation was a fitting response, but an employer may assert the Faragher affirmative defense unless the plaintiff quit in reasonable response to an adverse action officially changing their employment status or situation.
Direct evidence of discrimination is not required for a plaintiff to obtain a mixed motive jury instruction under Title VII.

Labor and Employment.case: Clackamas Gastroenterology Associates, P.C. v. Wells (2003)
Labor and Employment.summary: The common-law element of control is the principal guidepost in deciding whether director-shareholder physicians should be counted as employees for the purposes of the ADA.

Labor and Employment.case: Rush Prudential HMO, Inc. v. Moran (2002)
Labor and Employment.summary: HMOs may be insurers and thus subject to state regulation of insurers, and state laws directed toward the insurance industry are saved from preemption under ERISA's saving clause.

An agreement between an employer and an employee to arbitrate employment-related disputes does not bar the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) from pursuing victim-specific judicial relief in an ADA enforcement action.

Labor and Employment.case: Major League Baseball Players' Ass'n v. Garvey (2001)
Labor and Employment.summary: Judicial review of a labor arbitration decision is very limited, and courts are not authorized to review an arbitrator's decision on the merits despite allegations that the decision rests on factual errors or misinterprets the parties' agreement.

Labor and Employment.case: Clark County School District v. Breeden (2001)
Labor and Employment.summary: A retaliation claim will not withstand a summary judgment motion when nobody could reasonably believe that the incident of which the plaintiff complained violated Title VII.

Labor and Employment.case: Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Adams (2001)
Labor and Employment.summary: The exemption in Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, which excludes contracts of employment of seamen, railroad employees, or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce, is confined to transportation workers.
Mixed treatment and eligibility decisions by health maintenance organization (HMO) physicians are not fiduciary decisions under ERISA.

Labor and Employment.case: Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Products, Inc. (2000)
Labor and Employment.summary: A prima facie case of discrimination, combined with sufficient evidence for a reasonable jury to reject the employer's non-discriminatory explanation, may be adequate to sustain a finding of liability for intentional discrimination under the ADEA.

An employer's conduct does not need to be independently egregious for a punitive damages award in a Title VII case, but an employer may not be vicariously liable for managerial agents' discriminatory decisions when contrary to the employer's good-faith efforts to comply with Title VII.

Labor and Employment.case: Wright v. Universal Maritime Service Corp. (1998)
Labor and Employment.summary: For a union to waive employees' rights to a federal judicial forum for statutory anti-discrimination claims, the agreement to arbitrate these claims must be clear and unmistakable.

Labor and Employment.case: Faragher v. City of Boca Raton (1998)
Labor and Employment.summary: An employer is subject to vicarious liability to a victimized employee for an actionable hostile environment created by a supervisor, but may raise an affirmative defense if they exercised reasonable care to prevent and correct harassment and the plaintiff unreasonably failed to take advantage of preventive or corrective opportunities.

Under Title VII, an employee who refuses unwelcome and threatening sexual advances of a supervisor, yet suffers no adverse, tangible job consequences, may recover damages from the employer without showing employer negligence, but the employer may interpose an affirmative defense.

Labor and Employment.case: Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, Inc. (1998)
Labor and Employment.summary: Sex discrimination consisting of same-sex sexual harassment is actionable under Title VII.

Labor and Employment.case: Inter-Modal Rail Employees Ass'n v. Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe Railway Co. (1997)
Labor and Employment.summary: The power to amend or abolish an employee welfare benefit plan does not include the power to discriminate against the plan's participants and beneficiaries for the purpose of interfering with their attainment of rights under the plan.

The salary-basis test denies exempt status under the Fair Labor Standards Act when employees are covered by a policy that permits disciplinary or other deductions in pay as a practical matter, met if there is an actual practice or an employment policy creating a significant likelihood of such deductions.
When employers or other plan sponsors adopt, modify, or terminate pension plans, they do not act as fiduciaries but are analogous to settlors of a trust.

Labor and Employment.case: O'Connor v. Consolidated Coin Caterers Corp. (1996)
Labor and Employment.summary: The fact that one member of a protected class lost out to another member is irrelevant to an employment discrimination claim so long as they lost out because of their protected trait.

Labor and Employment.case: New York State Conference of Blue Cross & Blue Shield Plans v. Travelers Ins. Co. (1995)
Labor and Employment.summary: A law operating as an indirect source of merely economic influence on administrative decisions should not suffice to trigger ERISA preemption.

Labor and Employment.case: McKennon v. Nashville Banner Publishing Co. (1995)
Labor and Employment.summary: An employee discharged in violation of the ADEA is not barred from all relief when, after their discharge, their employer discovers evidence of wrongdoing that, in any event, would have led to their termination on lawful and legitimate grounds had the employer known of it.

Title VII is violated when the workplace is permeated with discriminatory behavior that is sufficiently severe or pervasive to create a discriminatorily hostile or abusive working environment, requiring an objectively hostile environment and the victim's subjective perception that the environment is abusive.
An employment decision based on years of service is not necessarily age-based, since this factor is analytically distinct from age.

Labor and Employment.case: Nationwide Mutual Ins. Co. v. Darden (1992)
Labor and Employment.summary: The common-law test for determining who qualifies as an employee under ERISA considers the hiring party's right to control the manner and means by which the product is accomplished, along with other factors such as skill, tools, work location, and discretion.
An ADEA claim can be subjected to compulsory arbitration.

ERISA's deemer clause excludes self-funded ERISA plans from the reach of the saving clause by relieving them from state laws purporting to regulate insurance, so state laws directed toward such plans are preempted.

Labor and Employment.case: Wards Cove Packing Co. v. Atonio (1989)
Labor and Employment.summary: The proper comparison in a disparate impact employment discrimination case is generally between the racial composition of the at-issue jobs and the racial composition of the qualified population in the relevant labor market.
In the specific context of sex stereotyping, an employer that acts on the basis of a belief that a woman cannot be aggressive, or that she must not be, has acted on the basis of gender.

A policy of paying discharged employees for their unused vacation time does not constitute an employee welfare benefit plan within the meaning of ERISA, and a criminal action to enforce that policy is not foreclosed.

Labor and Employment.case: Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch (1989)
Labor and Employment.summary: For disclosure purposes, a 'participant' in an ERISA plan means an employee in currently covered employment (or reasonably expected to be in currently covered employment), or a former employee who has a reasonable expectation of returning to covered employment or who has a colorable claim to vested benefits.

Labor and Employment.case: Watson v. Fort Worth Bank & Trust (1988)
Labor and Employment.summary: Disparate impact analysis in an employment discrimination claim may be applied to subjective employment criteria.

Labor and Employment.case: Communications Workers of America v. Beck (1988)
Labor and Employment.summary: The National Labor Relations Act does not permit a union, over the objections of dues-paying non-member employees, to expend funds collected from them on activities unrelated to collective bargaining activities.

Labor and Employment.case: Fort Halifax Packing Co., Inc. v. Coyne (1987)
Labor and Employment.summary: To be preempted by ERISA, a state statute must have some connection with or reference to an employee benefit plan.

Labor and Employment.case: Pilot Life Ins. Co. v. Dedeaux (1987)
Labor and Employment.summary: ERISA preempts state common law tort and contract actions asserting improper processing of a claim for benefits under an insured employee benefit plan.

Both the inception and the scope of the intrusion must be reasonable when a public employer intrudes on the constitutionally protected privacy interests of government employees for non-investigatory, work-related purposes, as well as for investigations of work-related misconduct.
A claim of hostile environment sexual harassment is a form of sex discrimination that is actionable under Title VII.

Labor and Employment.case: Shaw v. Delta Air Lines, Inc. (1983)
Labor and Employment.summary: A law relates to an employee benefit plan for ERISA purposes if it has a connection with or reference to such a plan.

When a public employee speaks as an employee on matters only of personal interest, a federal court is generally not the appropriate forum to review the wisdom of a personnel decision taken by a public agency allegedly in reaction to the employee's behavior.

A non-job-related test that has a disparate impact and is used to limit or classify employees is used to discriminate within the meaning of Title VII, even if it was not designed or intended to have this effect and even if an employer tries to compensate for its discriminatory effect.

Labor and Employment.case: Texas Dept. of Community Affairs v. Burdine (1981)
Labor and Employment.summary: When a plaintiff in a Title VII case has proved a prima facie case of employment discrimination, the defendant bears only the burden of explaining clearly the non-discriminatory reasons for its actions, which is sufficient if the defendant's evidence raises a genuine issue of fact as to whether it discriminated against the plaintiff.

A regulation could permit private employees of a private employer to avoid workplace conditions that they believe pose grave dangers to their own safety, when the employees have no power under the regulation to order their employer to correct the hazardous condition or to clear the dangerous workplace of others.

Labor and Employment.case: Givhan v. Western Line Consolidated School District (1979)
Labor and Employment.summary: A public employee does not forfeit their First Amendment protection when they arrange to communicate privately with their employer, rather than expressing their views publicly.

Once a prima facie case has been established by statistical workforce disparities, the employer must have an opportunity to show that the claimed discriminatory pattern was a product of pre-Title VII hiring, rather than unlawful post-Title VII discrimination.

To establish a prima facie case of employment discrimination, a plaintiff need only show that facially neutral standards select applicants for hire in a significantly discriminatory pattern, and if the employer proves the requirements are job-related, the plaintiff may show that other selection devices without a similar discriminatory effect would also serve the employer's legitimate interest.

In cases alleging a pattern or practice of employment discrimination, the government must show that discrimination was the standard operating procedure of the defendant, and statistics may be used in proving discrimination.

An employee's statutory right to trial de novo under Title VII is not foreclosed by prior submission of their claim to final arbitration under the non-discrimination clause of a collective bargaining agreement.

In a private, non-class action complaint under Title VII charging racial employment discrimination, the complainant has the burden of establishing a prima facie case by showing that they belong to a racial minority, they applied and were qualified for a job that the employer was trying to fill, they were rejected, and the employer continued to seek applicants with their qualifications.
An employment practice that operates to exclude members of a protected group is prohibited if it cannot be shown to be related to job performance, even if the employer lacked discriminatory intent.

When a public employee's false statements concerned issues that were currently the subject of public attention and did not interfere with the performance of their duties or the general operation of their employer, they were entitled to the same protection as if the statements had been made by a member of the general public.

Rulings, interpretations, and opinions of agency administrators are not controlling on courts but provide a body of experience and informed judgment that courts can use for guidance, and no principle of law precludes a determination that waiting time is working time under the Fair Labor Standards Act.

The fact that an employer has individual contracts of employment with a majority of its employees does not preclude the employees from exercising their right under the National Labor Relations Act to choose a representative for collective bargaining, nor does it warrant refusal by the employer to bargain with this representative regarding terms covered by the individual contracts.

When a strike is initiated and conducted in lawlessness by the seizure and retention of the employer's property, and the strikers are discharged because of their lawlessness, they do not remain employees under the NLRA.

Labor and Employment.case: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish (1937)
Labor and Employment.summary: A restraint or regulation of the liberty to contract is due process if it is reasonable in relation to its subject and adopted for the protection of the community against evils menacing the health, safety, morals, and welfare of the people.

A 1918 law in Washington, D.C. required employers to pay women and children a set minimum wage.The law was struck down as unconstitutional because it violated the freedom of contract guaranteed by the Fifth Amendment.

The regulation of the working hours of women falls within the police power of the state, and a statute directed exclusively to such regulation does not conflict with the Due Process or Equal Protection Clauses.

A U.S. court has no jurisdiction under the Thirteenth Amendment or other federal laws of a charge of conspiracy made and carried out in a state to prevent citizens of African descent, because of their race and color, from making or carrying out contracts and agreements to labor.

The general right to make a contract in relation to one's business is part of the liberty protected by the 14th Amendment, and this includes the right to purchase and sell labor, except as controlled by the state in the legitimate exercise of its police power.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: The Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment permits a flexible jurisdictional inquiry commensurate with the federal government's broader sovereign authority, rather than importing the 14th Amendment minimum contacts standard.

When an amendment to a complaint excises the federal-law claims that enabled removal, the federal court loses its supplemental jurisdiction over the related state-law claims, and the case must therefore return to state court.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Ford Motor Co. v. Montana Eighth Judicial District Court (2021)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: When a company serves a market for a product in a state, and that product causes an injury in the state to one of its residents, the state's courts may entertain the resulting lawsuit for specific jurisdiction.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Lucky Brand Dungarees, Inc. v. Marcel Fashions Group, Inc. (2020)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Any preclusion of defenses must, at a minimum, satisfy the strictures of issue preclusion or claim preclusion.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Court of California (2017)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Specific jurisdiction is lacking when there is no connection between the forum and the underlying controversy, regardless of the extent of a defendant's unconnected activities in the state.

A state court may exercise general jurisdiction over out-of-state corporations when their affiliations with the state are so continuous and systematic as to render them essentially at home in the forum state, consistent with the 14th Amendment's Due Process constraint.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. U.S. District Court for Western District of Texas (2013)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A forum selection clause may be enforced by a motion to transfer under Section 1404(a), and a district court should transfer the case unless extraordinary circumstances unrelated to the convenience of the parties clearly disfavor a transfer.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: J. McIntyre Machinery, Ltd. v. Nicastro (2011)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: The exercise of judicial power is not lawful unless the defendant purposefully avails itself of the privilege of conducting activities within the forum state, thus invoking the benefits and protections of its laws.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Goodyear Dunlop Tires Operations, S.A. v. Brown (2011)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Ties serving to bolster the exercise of specific jurisdiction do not warrant a determination that, based on those ties, the forum has general jurisdiction over a defendant.

A class in a proposed class action has common questions of law or fact if their claims depend on a common contention of such a nature that determination of its truth or falsity will resolve an issue that is central to the validity of each of the claims in one stroke.

For the purposes of federal diversity jurisdiction, a corporation's principal place of business refers to the place where its high level officers direct, control, and coordinate the corporation's activities (its nerve center).
The tenet that a court must accept as true all of the allegations contained in a complaint is inapplicable to legal conclusions.

The rule against non-party preclusion is subject to six categories of exceptions, including agreement to be bound, pre-existing legal relationships, adequate representation, control over litigation, relitigation through a proxy, and a special statutory scheme.

A plaintiff must plead enough facts to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face, calling for enough fact to raise a reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of an illegal agreement.

A court should not adopt a version of the facts for summary judgment when one story is blatantly contradicted by the record, and a police officer's attempt to terminate a dangerous high-speed car chase does not violate the Fourth Amendment.
When notice of a tax sale is mailed to the owner and returned undelivered, the government must take additional reasonable steps to provide notice before taking the owner's property.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Allapattah Services, Inc. (2005)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Supplemental jurisdiction is authorized over claims of other plaintiffs in the same case or controversy, even if their claims are for less than the requisite amount, when the other elements of jurisdiction are present, and at least one named plaintiff satisfies the amount-in-controversy requirement for diversity jurisdiction.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Grable & Sons Metal Products, Inc. v. Darue Engineering & Manufacturing (2005)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Jurisdiction over federal issues embedded in state law claims between non-diverse parties exists if the state law claim necessarily raises a stated federal issue, actually disputed and substantial, which a federal forum may entertain without disturbing any congressionally approved balance of federal and state judicial responsibilities.

An employment discrimination complaint need not contain specific facts establishing a prima facie case but instead must contain only a short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: J.E.B. v. Alabama ex rel. T.B. (1994)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: The Equal Protection Clause prohibits discrimination in jury selection on the basis of gender, or on the assumption that an individual will be biased in a particular case solely because that person happens to be a woman or a man.

Deciding what process must be afforded by a law enabling a private party to enlist the state's aid in depriving another party of their property through a prejudgment attachment involves three issues: the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation and value of additional safeguards, and the interest of the party seeking the remedy with regard for any ancillary interest of the government.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Edmonson v. Leesville Concrete Co., Inc. (1991)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A private litigant in a civil case may not use peremptory challenges to exclude jurors on account of race.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Carnival Cruise Lines, Inc. v. Shute (1991)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A non-negotiated forum clause in a passage contract may be enforceable even though it is not the subject of bargaining, although it is subject to judicial scrutiny for fundamental fairness.
It is not necessary for all joint tortfeasors to be named as defendants in a single lawsuit.

Service of process confers state court jurisdiction over a physically present non-resident, regardless of whether they were only briefly in the state or whether the cause of action is related to their activities there.

To determine whether a particular action will resolve legal (as opposed to equitable) rights, such that the plaintiff is entitled to a jury trial, courts must examine both the nature of the issues involved and, more importantly, the remedy sought.

A voluntary settlement in the form of a consent decree between one group of employees and their employer cannot possibly settle the conflicting claims of another group of employees who do not join in the agreement.
A grant of jurisdiction over claims involving particular parties does not confer jurisdiction over additional claims by or against different parties.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Asahi Metal Industry Co. v. Superior Court (1987)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: The substantial connection between a defendant and the forum state necessary for minimum contacts must derive from an action purposefully directed toward the forum state, as the mere placement of a product into the stream of commerce is not sufficient.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc. v. Thompson (1986)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A violation of a federal statute as an element of a state cause of action does not state a claim arising under federal law when Congress has determined that there should be no private federal cause of action for the violation.

Rule 56(c) mandates the entry of summary judgment against a party who fails to make a showing sufficient to establish the existence of an element essential to that party's case, and on which that party will bear the burden of proof at trial.

The inquiry involved in a ruling on a motion for summary judgment or for a directed verdict necessarily implicates the substantive evidentiary standard of proof that would apply at the trial on the merits.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Matsushita Electrical Industrial Co., Ltd. v. Zenith Radio Corp. (1986)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: To survive a motion for summary judgment, a plaintiff seeking damages for a violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act must present evidence that tends to exclude the possibility that the alleged conspirators acted independently, and the absence of any plausible motive to engage in the conduct charged is highly relevant to whether a genuine issue for trial exists.

Due process requires that absent class members in a class action receive notice, an opportunity to appear in person or by counsel, an opportunity to opt out, and adequate representation, but it does not require them to affirmatively opt in to the class.

A contract with an out-of-state party does not alone automatically establish sufficient minimum contacts for personal jurisdiction, but the court must evaluate prior negotiations, future consequences, contract terms, and the parties' course of dealing to determine purposeful establishment of minimum contacts.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Cooper v. Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond (1984)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A judgment in a class action determining that an employer did not engage in a general pattern or practice of racial discrimination against the certified class of employees does not preclude a class member from maintaining a subsequent civil action alleging an individual claim of racial discrimination against the employer.

Mere purchases, even if occurring at regular intervals, are not enough to warrant a state's assertion of general personal jurisdiction over a non-resident corporation in a cause of action not related to the purchases.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Insurance Corp. of Ireland v. Compagnie des Bauxites (1982)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Since the requirement that a court have personal jurisdiction protects an individual interest, it may be intentionally waived, or a defendant may be estopped from raising the issue for various reasons.

Plaintiffs may not defeat a motion to dismiss on the ground of forum non conveniens merely by showing that the substantive law that would be applied in the alternative forum is less favorable to them than the law of the chosen forum.

The attorney-client privilege exists to protect not only the giving of professional advice to those who can act on it, but also the giving of information to the lawyer to enable them to give sound and informed advice, but it only protects communications, not the underlying facts.

A state court could apply the law of its state when the state had a significant aggregation of contacts with the parties and the occurrence, creating state interests, such that the application of its law was neither arbitrary nor fundamentally unfair.

The foreseeability critical to due process analysis for personal jurisdiction is that the defendant's conduct and connection with the forum are such that they should reasonably anticipate being brought into court there.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Parklane Hosiery Co., Inc. v. Shore (1979)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A trial judge should not allow the use of offensive collateral estoppel if a plaintiff could easily have joined in the earlier action, or if the application of offensive estoppel would be unfair to the defendant.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Owen Equipment and Erection Co. v. Kroger (1978)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A finding that federal and non-federal claims arise from a common nucleus of operative fact does not suffice to establish that a federal court has the power to hear non-federal as well as federal claims, as the context in which a non-federal claim is asserted is crucial.

When the property serving as the basis for state court jurisdiction is completely unrelated to the plaintiff's cause of action, the presence of the property alone does not support jurisdiction without other ties among the defendant, the state, and the litigation.

A non-federal claim should not be the basis for joining a party over whom no independent federal jurisdiction exists, simply because that claim derives from the common nucleus of operative fact giving rise to the dispute between the parties to the federal claim.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Liberty Mutual Insurance Co. v. Wetzel (1976)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: An order constituting a grant of partial summary judgment limited to liability cannot be considered final when damages or other relief remain to be resolved.

Identifying the specific dictates of due process generally requires considering the private interest affected, the risk and value of additional procedural safeguards, and the government's interest and administrative burdens.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. (1972)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: A forum selection clause that was a vital part of a towing contract was binding unless the party challenging its enforcement could meet the heavy burden of showing that its enforcement would be unreasonable, unfair, or unjust.
From the standpoint of due process, it is immaterial that a deprivation of property may be temporary and non-final.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: Blonder Tongue v. University of Illinois Foundation (1971)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Res judicata and collateral estoppel are affirmative defenses that must be pleaded to give the opposing party notice of the plea of estoppel and a chance to argue why the imposition of estoppel would be inappropriate.

A party moving for summary judgment has the burden of showing the absence of a genuine issue as to any material fact, and the material submitted by the moving party must be viewed in the light most favorable to the opposing party.
A pre-termination evidentiary hearing is necessary to provide a recipient of welfare benefits with procedural due process, as the recipient's interest clearly outweighs the state's competing concern.

Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.case: United Mine Workers of America v. Gibbs (1966)
Lawsuits & Legal Procedures.summary: Pendent jurisdiction exists whenever there is a substantial federal claim, and the relationship between the federal claim and the asserted state claims permits the conclusion that the entire action before the court comprises one case because the state and federal claims must derive from a common nucleus of operative fact.

When actions were properly brought in the transferor district court, and the defendants seek transfer under Section 1404(a), the change of venue should not be accompanied by a change in the governing state laws.
